## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Mathematical Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Jn equals 0 in Equilibrium"]:::sNode
    S2["Expand Jn equals qmun E plus qDn dn by dx"]:::sNode
    S3["Solve for E equals negative Dn over mun times 1 over n times dn by dx"]:::sNode
    S4["Apply Einstein Relation Dn over mun equals VT"]:::sNode
    S5["Result: E equals negative VT over n times dn by dx"]:::coreNode
    S6["Assume n of x equals N0 exp x over LD"]:::sNode
    S7["Compute dn by dx equals n of x over LD"]:::sNode
    S8["Substitute and Cancel n of x"]:::sNode
    S9["Final Result: E equals VT over LD (constant)"]:::finalNode
    S10["Integrate: phi of x equals VT over LD times x"]:::finalNode

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
```

> **How to Read These Diagrams in the Exam Hall**
> Each Mermaid block is not a substitute for a hand-drawn sketch on your answer sheet, but a **logical roadmap** for structuring your written answer. KTU examiners reward answers that explicitly follow a clear logical sequence — these flows give you exactly that structure.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Define the **thermal voltage** $V_T$ and write its numerical value at room temperature. State the **Einstein relation** connecting mobility and diffusion coefficient.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The thermal voltage is defined as

$$V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$$

where $k$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $q$ is the electronic charge. At $T = 300\,\text{K}$,

$$V_T = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$$

The Einstein relation is

$$D_n = \mu_n V_T \quad \text{(for electrons)} \qquad D_p = \mu_p V_T \quad \text{(for holes)}$$

This relation ties the **diffusion coefficient** to the **mobility**, both arising from the same thermal agitation of carriers. **[3 marks]**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Why does a **non-uniformly doped** semiconductor exhibit a built-in electric field even in the absence of any external bias?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In a non-uniformly doped semiconductor, the mobile carrier concentration $n(x)$ varies with position. Carriers spontaneously **diffuse** from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration. Because the dopant ions themselves are **immobile**, this diffusion leaves behind a region of net positive charge (uncovered donor ions) on the high-concentration side and a net negative charge (accumulated electrons) on the low-concentration side. This charge separation generates an internal electric field $E(x)$ via Poisson's equation. The field grows until the **drift current** it produces on the carriers exactly cancels the **diffusion current**, after which no further net flow occurs and equilibrium is reached. The field thus exists purely due to the doping gradient, independent of any external bias. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> Consider an n-type silicon semiconductor with an **exponential doping profile**:
> $$N_D(x) = N_0 \exp(x/L_D)$$
> where $N_0 = 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3}$ and $L_D = 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$.
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **built-in electric field** $E(x)$ at thermal equilibrium. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the magnitude of this field at $T = 300\,\text{K}$ and the **electrostatic potential difference** over a length of $L_D$. Comment on its engineering significance. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — State the equilibrium current condition**

In thermal equilibrium, the net electron current density $J_n = 0$:

$$J_n = q\mu_n n(x) E(x) + q D_n \dfrac{dn(x)}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the field**

$$E(x) = -\dfrac{D_n}{\mu_n}\,\dfrac{1}{n(x)}\,\dfrac{dn(x)}{dx}$$

Using Einstein relation $D_n/\mu_n = V_T$:

$$E(x) = -\dfrac{V_T}{n(x)}\,\dfrac{dn(x)}{dx} \qquad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the derivative for exponential profile**

For $n(x) \approx N_D(x) = N_0 \exp(x/L_D)$:

$$\dfrac{dn}{dx} = \dfrac{N_0}{L_D}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{x}{L_D}\right) = \dfrac{n(x)}{L_D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back**

$$E(x) = -\dfrac{V_T}{n(x)} \cdot \dfrac{n(x)}{L_D} = -\dfrac{V_T}{L_D}$$

**Result:** The built-in electric field is **uniform** with magnitude $E = V_T / L_D$. The negative sign indicates it points in the $-x$ direction (opposing the diffusion of electrons toward higher concentration). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the thermal voltage**

At $T = 300\,\text{K}$:

$$V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.585 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{V} \approx 25.85\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the built-in field magnitude**

$$|E| = \dfrac{V_T}{L_D} = \dfrac{25.85 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}} = 5.17 \times 10^{4}\,\text{V/m} \approx 51.7\,\text{kV/m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute potential difference over $L_D$**

Since $\phi(x)$ varies linearly with slope $V_T/L_D$, over a length $L_D$:

$$\Delta\phi = |E| \cdot L_D = V_T = 25.85\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering significance**

- The field of ~$52\,\text{kV/m}$ is comparable to applied fields in nanoscale MOSFETs and is sufficient to drift carriers effectively over sub-micron distances.
- The 26 mV potential drop over $L_D$ is on the order of $V_T$, meaning carriers gain appreciable drift energy even without external bias — this is the basis of **built-in drift fields** in HBT bases.
- In solar cells, such built-in fields from graded emitter doping help separate photocarriers before they recombine, boosting quantum efficiency. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Starting from the electron current density expression, show that the **internal electric field** in a non-uniformly doped semiconductor at thermal equilibrium is given by $E(x) = -\dfrac{V_T}{n}\,\dfrac{dn}{dx}$. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** For a silicon sample at 300 K with electron concentration varying as $n(x) = 10^{15}\exp(2\times 10^{4}x)\,\text{cm}^{-3}$, determine: **(i)** the built-in electric field at $x = 0$, and **(ii)** the spatial derivative of the field $dE/dx$. Comment on the sign and physical meaning. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Write total electron current**

$$J_n(x) = q\mu_n n(x)E(x) + qD_n \dfrac{dn(x)}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition $J_n = 0$**

$$q\mu_n n E + qD_n \dfrac{dn}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $E$**

$$E = -\dfrac{D_n}{\mu_n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{n}\cdot\dfrac{dn}{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Einstein relation**

$$\dfrac{D_n}{\mu_n} = V_T$$

$$\boxed{\,E(x) = -\dfrac{V_T}{n(x)}\dfrac{dn(x)}{dx}\,}$$

The negative sign shows that the field is directed opposite to the direction of carrier concentration increase. It is a self-consistent field arising from charge separation due to diffusion of mobile carriers leaving behind immobile ions. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 marks]**

Given:

$$n(x) = 10^{15}\exp(2 \times 10^{4}x)\,\text{cm}^{-3}, \quad T = 300\,\text{K}$$

**Part (i): Field at $x = 0$**

Compute the derivative:

$$\dfrac{dn}{dx} = 10^{15} \cdot 2 \times 10^{4}\exp(2\times 10^{4}x) = 2 \times 10^{19}\exp(2\times 10^{4}x)\,\text{cm}^{-4}$$

So

$$\dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dx} = 2 \times 10^{4}\,\text{cm}^{-1} = 2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

This is constant (independent of $x$).

Field magnitude:

$$E(x) = -V_T \cdot \dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dx} = -0.02585 \times 2 \times 10^{6} = -5.17 \times 10^{4}\,\text{V/m}$$

At $x = 0$:

$$\boxed{\,E(0) \approx -51.7\,\text{kV/m}\,}$$

**Part (ii): Derivative of field**

Since $\dfrac{1}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dx} = 2\times 10^{6}\,\text{m}^{-1}$ is constant, $E(x)$ itself is **constant**:

$$\dfrac{dE}{dx} = 0$$

**Physical meaning:**

- The **negative sign** indicates the field points in the $-x$ direction, opposing the diffusion of electrons from high to low concentration.
- $dE/dx = 0$ implies **uniform space-charge density** throughout this region (consistent with the constant logarithmic derivative), meaning $\rho(x) = \varepsilon_s \cdot dE/dx = 0$ — a subtle result suggesting this particular exponential profile produces a self-consistent charge-neutral equilibrium on the macroscopic scale (within the depletion-free approximation). **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Sign errors on the built-in field equation**: Many students write $E = +\dfrac{V_T}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dx}$ instead of the correct **negative sign**. The minus sign is essential — without it, you are claiming the field *aids* diffusion, which violates the second law of thermodynamics.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Einstein relation**: When asked to derive $E(x)$, students sometimes treat $D_n$ and $\mu_n$ as independent. They are **not** — always substitute $D_n = \mu_n V_T$ before finalizing the result.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $n(x)$ and $N_D(x)$**: In a non-degenerate n-type semiconductor at equilibrium, $n(x) \approx N_D(x)$ (charge neutrality in the bulk), but **only when the Debye length is much smaller than the doping length scale**. Examiners may deduct marks if you blindly equate them without stating this assumption.
>
> 4. **Units mismatch in numerical problems**: $V_T$ must be in **volts** (not mV), and $L_D$ must be in **meters** (not $\mu$m). Converting $25.85\,\text{mV} = 0.02585\,\text{V}$ before substituting is a common trip-point.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on physical meaning**: KTU awards marks for **interpretation**, not just derivation. Always end with a one-line physical insight: what does the sign mean? what does the magnitude imply for device behavior?

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Non-uniform doping** means $N_D(x)$ or $N_A(x)$ varies with position — typical in ion-implanted nanoscale devices.
- The **built-in electric field** arises from charge separation when mobile carriers diffuse and leave behind immobile ions.
- The governing relation is $E(x) = -\dfrac{V_T}{n}\dfrac{dn}{dx}$, derived from setting net current $J_n = 0$ at equilibrium.
- The **Einstein relation** $D = \mu V_T$ connects diffusion and mobility — they have the **same physical origin** (thermal agitation).
- For an **exponential doping profile** $n(x) = N_0 \exp(x/L_D)$, the built-in field is **constant**: $E = V_T/L_D$.
- The corresponding electrostatic potential is **linear**: $\phi(x) = (V_T/L_D)\,x$.
- The thermal voltage at 300 K is $V_T \approx \mathbf{25.85\,\text{mV}}$ — memorize this number.
- Engineering applications include **HBT base drift fields**, **graded-emitter solar cells**, **LDMOS drift regions**, and **avalanche photodiodes**.
- Always include the **negative sign** in the field equation; it signifies the field opposes diffusion.
- For numerical problems, ensure **unit consistency**: $V_T$ in volts, lengths in meters, doping in $\text{m}^{-3}$.
- The transport equation $J_n = q\mu_n n E + qD_n(dn/dx)$ is the **master equation** of carrier flow in semiconductors — it appears in nearly every KTU nanoelectronics question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Threshold Voltage Scaling — Section 1: Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In nano-scale Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs), the **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)** is rigorously defined as the minimum gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) that must be applied to induce a surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ (twice the Fermi potential) at the semiconductor-oxide interface. This condition corresponds to the onset of strong inversion, where a continuous, conducting inversion layer (channel) is formed between the source and drain terminals.

Mathematically, the **long-channel (classical) threshold voltage** for an n-MOSFET is expressed as:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma \cdot \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

where:
- $V_{FB}$ = Flat-band voltage
- $\phi_F$ = Fermi potential $\left(\frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)\right)$
- $\gamma$ = Body-effect coefficient
- $V_{SB}$ = Source-to-body (substrate) bias
- $N_A$ = Channel doping concentration
- $n_i$ = Intrinsic carrier concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Threshold voltage scaling is **the central design lever** in nano-CMOS design. It directly trades off **performance (speed)** against **leakage power (standby current)**. Every nanometer of oxide thinning and every volt of $V_{DD}$ reduction cascades into a re-engineering of $V_{th}$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Dam and Valve" Model

Imagine a **hydroelectric dam** standing between two large reservoirs of water (the **source** and **drain**). The dam wall represents the **potential barrier** created by the gate-controlled depletion region.

- **Gate Voltage = The Sluice Gate Operator.** When the operator (gate) opens the sluice gate, water (electrons) can flow. The minimum force the operator must apply to start the water flowing is the **threshold voltage**.
- **Below $V_{th}$:** A tiny amount of water still seeps through cracks — this is the **subthreshold leakage current** $I_{off}$.
- **Above $V_{th}$:** A torrent of water flows, generating useful power — this is the **ON-state current** $I_{on}$.
- **Scaling the Dam Lower (Reducing $V_{th}$):** More water flows (faster switching), but more leakage seeps through (higher static power).
- **Scaling the Dam Higher (Raising $V_{th}$):** Almost no leakage, but the gate operator must work harder and slower (slower circuit).

This trade-off is the **heart of threshold voltage scaling**.

## 1.3 Why Threshold Voltage Scaling Matters in Nanoelectronics

As CMOS technology entered the **deep sub-micrometer (DSM)** and **nano-scale** regimes, the international technology community — guided by Moore's Law and the **ITRS / IRDS (International Roadmap for Devices and Systems)** — faced a paradox:

> [!NOTE]
> To improve speed and density, supply voltage $V_{DD}$ had to be reduced (from 5 V in the 1980s down to ~0.7 V in modern FinFET nodes). However, if $V_{th}$ were kept constant, the available **over-drive voltage** $(V_{DD} - V_{th})$ — which determines drive current — would collapse, killing performance. Therefore, $V_{th}$ had to be scaled **in lockstep** with $V_{DD}$.

Consequences of aggressive $V_{th}$ scaling:
- **Exponential growth in subthreshold leakage** (every ~60 mV reduction in $V_{th}$ at 300 K increases $I_{off}$ by ~10×).
- **Increased sensitivity to process variations** (random dopant fluctuations, line-edge roughness, quantum confinement).
- **Emergence of short-channel and quantum effects** that distort the classical $V_{th}$ picture.

**Standard Reference Metrics (to memorize for KTU):**
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85~mV}$
- **Subthreshold Swing (60 mV/decade limit):** $S = 60 \text{ mV/decade}$ at $T = 300$ K
- **Typical scaled $V_{th}$ (modern logic):** $\mathbf{0.2~V~to~0.4~V}$ for high-performance, $\mathbf{0.4~V~to~0.6~V}$ for low-power.

# Threshold Voltage Scaling — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Components of the Classical Threshold Voltage

The long-channel $V_{th}$ consists of **three additive components**, each with a distinct physical origin:

1. **Flat-Band Voltage ($V_{FB}$)** — The voltage required to make the energy bands flat across the MOS structure. It compensates the work-function difference between the gate and the semiconductor, and any fixed/trapped oxide charges.

$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \frac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$$

2. **Surface Potential Term ($2\phi_F$)** — Twice the Fermi potential. This is the electrostatic potential drop across the depletion layer at the onset of strong inversion.

3. **Voltage Drop Across the Oxide ($\frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$)** — The contribution from the depletion region charge that must be supported by the gate field.

## 2.2 Body Effect (Reverse Bias Sensitivity)

When the source is biased above the body ($V_{SB} > 0$), the source-body junction becomes reverse-biased, **widening the depletion region** and **increasing $V_{th}$**. The sensitivity is governed by the **body-effect coefficient $\gamma$**:

$$\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

The body-effect term causes $V_{th}$ to rise as:

$$\Delta V_{th,body} = \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

## 2.3 Short-Channel Effects on $V_{th}$

As channel length $L$ approaches the depletion-region widths, the classical square-law picture collapses. Two dominant effects emerge:

### 2.3.1 Threshold Voltage Roll-Off
$V_{th}$ **decreases** as $L$ shrinks below a critical length, because the gate loses electrostatic control over the channel — the source and drain depletion regions "share" the channel charge.

Empirical model:

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{th,\infty} - \Delta V_{th,roll-off}(L)$$

where $V_{th,\infty}$ is the long-channel value and $\Delta V_{th,roll-off}$ grows roughly as $1/L$ or exponentially with $L$.

### 2.3.2 Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)
At high $V_{DS}$, the drain's electric field **penetrates the channel** and lowers the source-side potential barrier, effectively **reducing $V_{th}$** — even though the gate has not changed.

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

where $\eta_{DIBL}$ is the DIBL coefficient (units: V/V), typically a few hundred mV/V in nanometer CMOS.

### 2.3.3 Reverse Short-Channel Effect (RSCE)
Caused by non-uniform channel doping (e.g., **halo/pocket implants** used in modern processes). The local $V_{th}$ near the source/drain is **higher** than in the channel center, producing an apparent $V_{th}$ **increase** at short $L$ — a counter-effect that engineers actually exploit to combat roll-off.

## 2.4 Subthreshold Swing and the $V_{th}$–Leakage Trade-off

The **subthreshold swing (S)** quantifies how sharply the transistor turns off. It sets the floor on $V_{th}$ scaling because:

$$I_D \approx I_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{S}} \quad \text{(subthreshold regime)}$$

The classical (Boltzmann-limited) swing is:

$$S = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right) \geq 60~\text{mV/decade at 300 K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 60 mV/decade barrier** is a *thermodynamic* limit set by Boltzmann statistics. To break it, the industry moved to **multi-gate FETs (FinFETs, GAAFETs)** and is now exploring **negative-capacitance FETs (NCFETs)** and **tunnel FETs (TFETs)**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Long-channel $V_{th}$ | $V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$ | Onset of strong inversion | V |
| 2 | Fermi potential | $\phi_F = \frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Energy gap between $E_F$ and intrinsic level | V |
| 3 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Sensitivity of $V_{th}$ to body bias | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | Oxide capacitance | $C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m$^2$ |
| 5 | DIBL shift | $\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$ | Threshold reduction due to drain field | V |
| 6 | Subthreshold swing | $S = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | Gate efficiency in turning off | mV/decade |
| 7 | Subthreshold current | $I_D = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V_{GS}-V_{th})}{nkT}\right)\left(1-\exp\!\left(-\frac{qV_{DS}}{kT}\right)\right)$ | Leakage in weak inversion | A |
| 8 | Off-current per decade | $I_{off} \propto 10^{-V_{th}/S}$ | Static leakage budget | A |
| 9 | Over-drive voltage | $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = V_{DD} - V_{th}$ (max) | Available drive headroom | V |
| 10 | Quantum threshold shift | $\Delta V_{th,QM} \approx \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2m^* t_{inv}^2 q}$ | Effect of carrier confinement in ultra-thin bodies | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the working tools for **digital cell library characterization** in EDA tools (Cadence, Synopsys, Mentor). Every standard cell in a modern processor is characterized by a specific $V_{th}$ class — LVT (Low), SVT (Standard), HVT (High) — to balance timing, power, and process variation.

## 2.6 Multi-$V_{th}$ Design — A Practical Scaling Strategy

Rather than picking one $V_{th}$ for the entire chip, modern ICs use **multiple threshold voltages** in a single die:

- **LVT (Low $V_{th}$):** Critical-path logic — fast but leaky.
- **SVT (Standard $V_{th}$):** Generic logic — balanced.
- **HVT (High $V_{th}$):** Sleep transistors and non-critical paths — slow but ultra-low leakage.

This is combined with **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB)** or **Forward/Reverse Body Biasing (FBB/RBB)** to dynamically tune $V_{th}$ post-fabrication — recovering performance and containing leakage simultaneously.

# Threshold Voltage Scaling — Section 3: Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Long-Channel Threshold Voltage

**Starting point:** Gauss's law applied to the MOS structure at the surface.

**Step 1 — Condition for Strong Inversion.**
By definition, strong inversion begins when the surface electron concentration equals the bulk doping $N_A$. From semiconductor electrostatics:

$$\phi_s(\text{strong inversion}) = 2\phi_F = \frac{2kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

This is the surface potential at threshold.

**Step 2 — Charge in the Depletion Region.**
The bulk charge (per unit area) at inversion is:

$$Q_B = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

This is obtained by integrating Poisson's equation across the depletion width $W_D = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{qN_A}}$.

**Step 3 — Voltage Balance Across the MOS Stack.**
The applied gate voltage $V_{GS}$ must support three contributions: the flat-band voltage drop, the surface potential, and the voltage across the oxide due to the depletion charge:

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s + \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and $Q_B$.**
Replacing the surface potential and bulk charge:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 5 — Factoring out $\gamma$ to expose the body-effect term.**
Recognizing that $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$:

$$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)}$$

This is the **canonical long-channel MOSFET threshold voltage** used universally in textbooks and EDA tools.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Subthreshold Swing

In the subthreshold regime, the drain current is dominated by **diffusion** (not drift), and the channel acts as a series of diodes back-to-back. The current is:

$$I_D = \mu_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot C_{ox} \cdot (n-1) \cdot V_T^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)\left(1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right)$$

where $n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}$ is the **body-effect factor** (ideality coefficient).

**Step 1 — Logarithmic Derivative.**
The subthreshold swing is defined as the change in $V_{GS}$ required to change $I_D$ by one decade (factor of 10):

$$S = \left(\frac{d(\log_{10} I_D)}{dV_{GS}}\right)^{-1} = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\ln I_D)}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiating the Exponential.**
Since $I_D \propto \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS}}{nV_T}\right)$, we have $\frac{d(\ln I_D)}{dV_{GS}} = \frac{1}{nV_T}$.

**Step 3 — Final Expression.**

$$S = \ln(10) \cdot n V_T = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluation at Room Temperature (T = 300 K).**
With $\frac{kT}{q} = 25.85$ mV, $\ln(10) = 2.3026$, and the ideal case $C_{dep} \to 0$:

$$S_{min} = 2.3026 \times 25.85~\text{mV} \approx 59.6~\text{mV/decade} \approx \mathbf{60~mV/decade}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** The 60 mV/decade limit arises because the gate field must compete with the **semiconductor's own thermal energy** $kT$. To break this limit, the gate must control the channel potential more efficiently than thermal diffusion — achievable only through **multi-gate geometry**, **ferroelectric negative capacitance**, or **band-to-band tunneling (TFETs)**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the DIBL-Induced $V_{th}$ Shift

**Step 1 — Physical Picture.**
In a short-channel device, the drain depletion region extends into the channel. The drain potential $V_{DS}$ couples capacitively into the **mid-channel potential barrier** that controls injection from the source.

**Step 2 — Coupling Model.**
Treat the source-side barrier as a node that is capacitively pulled down by $V_{DS}$ through a divider formed by the gate-oxide capacitance and the total (oxide + depletion + drain-junction) capacitance:

$$\Delta\phi_{barrier} = -\frac{C_{drain}}{C_{total}} \cdot V_{DS}$$

**Step 3 — Identification of the DIBL Coefficient.**
Define $\eta_{DIBL} = \frac{C_{drain}}{C_{total}}$ (a geometry- and doping-dependent parameter, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ V/V in nanoscale devices).

**Step 4 — Threshold Voltage Shift.**
Since $V_{th}$ is defined by a fixed barrier height, a barrier reduction of $\Delta\phi_{barrier}$ directly subtracts from $V_{th}$:

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Mitigation:** DIBL is suppressed by (i) increasing channel doping, (ii) reducing junction depths (raised source/drain), and (iii) improving electrostatic integrity via thinner $t_{ox}$, higher-$k$ dielectrics, and multi-gate structures (FinFET, GAA).

## 3.4 Threshold Voltage Roll-Off (Charge Sharing Model)

**Step 1 — Geometry.**
Assume a long-channel device with rectangular depletion regions of width $W_{D,max}$. In a short-channel device, the source/drain depletion regions **truncate** the channel depletion, reducing the total charge that the gate must support.

**Step 2 — Effective Bulk Charge Factor.**
Define the fraction $F$ of the bulk charge still under gate control:

$$F \approx 1 - \frac{W_{D,source} + W_{D,drain}}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Voltage Expression with Charge Sharing.**

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma \cdot F \cdot \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$

**Step 4 — Roll-Off Magnitude.**

$$\Delta V_{th,roll-off} = V_{th,\infty} - V_{th}(L) = \gamma \cdot (1 - F) \cdot \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$$

As $L \to 2 W_{D,max}$, $F \to 0$ and $V_{th} \to V_{FB} + 2\phi_F$, leading to a catastrophic loss of gate control — this defines the **minimum channel length** for the technology.

# Threshold Voltage Scaling — Section 5: KTU 2024 Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Threshold Voltage in a MOSFET and state its significance in nano-CMOS scaling. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Threshold voltage $V_{th}$ is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) between the source and drain, corresponding to the onset of strong inversion where the surface potential equals $2\phi_F$.

**Significance in nano-CMOS scaling:**
1. As supply voltage $V_{DD}$ is scaled down for power reduction, $V_{th}$ must also scale to preserve sufficient over-drive voltage $(V_{DD} - V_{th})$ and hence maintain drive current and switching speed.
2. Aggressive $V_{th}$ scaling causes **exponential growth in subthreshold leakage current** $I_{off}$ (every 60 mV reduction ≈ 10× increase in leakage at 300 K).
3. Thus $V_{th}$ is the central design parameter that **balances speed, power, and leakage** in scaled CMOS technology.

### Question 2: What is Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL) and how does it affect threshold voltage? `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**DIBL** is a short-channel effect in which the electric field from the drain terminal penetrates the channel and reduces the source-side potential barrier, effectively lowering the threshold voltage as $V_{DS}$ increases.

**Effect on $V_{th}$:**

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

- At high $V_{DS}$, $V_{th}$ is reduced, increasing both ON current and OFF leakage.
- DIBL worsens as channel length shrinks, making it a key limiter of MOSFET scalability.
- It is mitigated by improving electrostatic integrity: thinner gate oxide, higher channel doping, shallow junctions, and multi-gate architectures (FinFET, GAA).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Derive the long-channel threshold voltage equation for an n-MOSFET, clearly explaining the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Condition for strong inversion:**
At the onset of strong inversion, the surface electron concentration equals the bulk doping $N_A$, giving a surface potential of:
$$\phi_s = 2\phi_F = \frac{2kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Bulk depletion charge:**
Solving Poisson's equation across the depletion width $W_D = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{qN_A}}$ yields:
$$Q_B = qN_A W_D = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage partition across the MOS stack:**
Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law:
$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + \phi_s + \frac{Q_B}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting at threshold:**
$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

**Step 5 — Introducing the body-effect coefficient $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$:**
$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $V_{FB}$: Voltage to flatten the bands, accounting for gate-semiconductor work-function mismatch and oxide charge.
- $2\phi_F$: Bulk band-bending required to bring the surface into strong inversion.
- $\gamma(\cdots)$: Voltage drop across the oxide needed to support the depletion-region charge; sensitive to substrate bias via $V_{SB}$ (body effect).

**b) Explain short-channel effects on threshold voltage: roll-off, DIBL, and reverse short-channel effect. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Threshold Voltage Roll-Off:**
As channel length $L$ decreases, the source and drain depletion regions occupy an increasing fraction of the channel. The gate therefore loses control over a portion of the bulk charge — a phenomenon called **charge sharing**. This reduces the $V_{th}$:

$$V_{th}(L) = V_{th,\infty} - \Delta V_{th,roll-off}(L)$$

The roll-off becomes catastrophic when $L$ approaches $2W_{D,max}$.

**2. Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL):**
At high $V_{DS}$, the drain's electric field penetrates the channel and lowers the source-side potential barrier. This effectively reduces $V_{th}$ even with fixed $V_{GS}$:

$$\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

DIBL increases leakage, degrades output resistance, and is a major reliability concern at short $L$.

**3. Reverse Short-Channel Effect (RSCE):**
Modern processes use **halo (pocket) implants** near source/drain to suppress roll-off. The resulting non-uniform channel doping causes a localized $V_{th}$ rise near the junctions. When $L$ shrinks, the halos overlap and the **average** $V_{th}$ increases — the opposite of roll-off. RSCE is exploited deliberately to fine-tune the $V_{th}$ profile.

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**a) Derive the subthreshold swing equation. Why is 60 mV/decade a fundamental limit, and how do modern devices attempt to overcome it? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Subthreshold current expression:**
In the weak-inversion regime, drain current is dominated by diffusion:
$$I_D \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{nV_T}\right)$$
with $n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}$ and $V_T = kT/q$.

**Step 2 — Definition of subthreshold swing:**
$$S \triangleq \left(\frac{\partial \log_{10} I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\right)^{-1} = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{\partial V_{GS}}{\partial \ln I_D} = \ln(10) \cdot n V_T$$

**Step 3 — Final expression:**
$$S = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Boltzmann limit at 300 K:**
With $C_{dep} \to 0$ and $T = 300$ K:
$$S_{min} = 2.3026 \times 25.85~\text{mV} \approx 60~\text{mV/decade}$$

**Why is 60 mV/decade a fundamental limit?**
The limit is imposed by **Boltzmann thermodynamics**: the carrier population in the channel follows an exponential energy distribution $\exp(-E/kT)$. The gate modulates the surface potential, but thermal carriers always populate states with energy $\sim kT$ above the band edge. Hence a change of $\sim 2.3 \cdot kT/q$ in surface potential is the minimum required to swing the current by one decade — a fact independent of device geometry, doping, or material.

**How modern devices attempt to overcome it:**
1. **Multi-gate MOSFETs (FinFET, GAAFET):** Improve the ratio $C_{ox}/C_{dep}$, reducing $n$ toward 1.
2. **Negative-Capacitance FETs (NCFETs):** Insert a ferroelectric layer to provide **internal voltage amplification**, achieving $S < 60$ mV/dec experimentally.
3. **Tunnel FETs (TFETs):** Use band-to-band tunneling as the injection mechanism, which is a sharper energy-filtering process than thermal injection.
4. **Cold-source FETs and Impact-Ionization MOS (I-MOS):** Exploit non-Boltzmann injection mechanisms.

**b) Discuss threshold voltage scaling strategies in modern nano-CMOS, including multi-$V_{th}$ design, adaptive body biasing, and the impact of process variations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for $V_{th}$ Scaling:**
To keep pace with Moore's Law, $V_{DD}$ has been reduced from 5 V to sub-1 V. Maintaining a constant $V_{th}$ would collapse the over-drive $(V_{DD} - V_{th})$, killing performance. Therefore, $V_{th}$ must be scaled in step with $V_{DD}$.

**2. Multi-$V_{th}$ Design:**
Modern cell libraries offer multiple threshold flavors:
- **LVT (Low $V_{th}$):** Used on critical timing paths — high speed, high leakage.
- **SVT (Standard $V_{th}$):** General logic — balanced trade-off.
- **HVT (High $V_{th}$):** Non-critical paths and sleep transistors — low leakage.

This **heterogeneous integration** allows chip-level optimization of the speed-power curve.

**3. Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB):**
By modulating $V_{SB}$ dynamically:
$$V_{th}(V_{SB}) = V_{th,0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

- **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** raises $V_{th}$ and reduces leakage in idle mode.
- **Forward Body Bias (FBB)** lowers $V_{th}$ and boosts speed in active mode.
A closed-loop monitor (e.g., ring oscillator) senses the actual speed and adjusts the body bias, compensating for **process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations**.

**4. Impact of Process Variations:**
At nanometer nodes, $V_{th}$ variation is dominated by:
- **Random Dopant Fluctuations (RDF):** Atomistic uncertainty in channel doping.
- **Line-Edge Roughness (LER):** Lithographic imperfections.
- **Fin/Gate thickness variation (FinFET):** Geometric non-uniformity.
- **Negative-Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI):** Aging-induced $V_{th}$ shift in p-MOS.

Strategies to mitigate variation include: undoped or lightly doped channels (FinFET), multi-$V_{th}$ design, ABB, and statistical timing analysis in EDA flows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the body-effect term structure:** Writing $V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2q\varepsilon_{si}N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ without including the substrate-bias dependence $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$ is a frequent error. Always show the **complete** expression with the $\gamma(\cdots)$ form when $V_{SB} \neq 0$.
> 2. **Mixing DIBL with roll-off:** Roll-off is a function of $V_{DS} = 0$ behavior versus $L$, while DIBL is the $V_{DS}$-dependent reduction. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping the units in the subthreshold swing derivation:** Always state $S$ in mV/decade and substitute $T = 300$ K explicitly to earn the closing marks.
> 4. **Confusing 60 mV/decade with a "technological" limit:** It is a **thermodynamic Boltzmann limit**, not just a manufacturing constraint. Examiners will deduct marks for stating it as a mere engineering bottleneck.
> 5. **In the derivation questions, missing the Gauss's-law/Poisson's-equation step:** Simply writing the final formula without showing the surface-potential condition $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and the depletion-charge integration loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **$V_{th}$ definition:** Minimum $V_{GS}$ to achieve $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ (strong inversion onset).
- **Long-channel $V_{th}$:** $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- **Body-effect coefficient:** $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}}{C_{ox}}$; controls $V_{th}$ sensitivity to substrate bias.
- **Short-channel roll-off:** $V_{th}$ decreases as $L$ shrinks (charge sharing).
- **DIBL:** $V_{th}$ decreases as $V_{DS}$ rises — $\Delta V_{th,DIBL} = -\eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$.
- **RSCE:** Local halos raise $V_{th}$ near junctions, counter-acting roll-off.
- **Subthreshold swing:** $S = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \cdot (1 + C_{dep}/C_{ox}) \geq 60$ mV/decade at 300 K.
- **Leakage rule of thumb:** Every 60 mV reduction in $V_{th}$ increases $I_{off}$ by ~10× at 300 K.
- **Scaling strategies:** Multi-$V_{th}$ cells, adaptive body biasing, multi-gate (FinFET/GAA) structures, NCFET and TFET steep-slope devices.
- **Variation sources:** Random dopant fluctuations, LER, fin/thickness variation, NBTI aging.
- **Modern $V_{th}$ range:** ~0.2 V (LVT) to ~0.5 V (HVT) at 7 nm and below.
- **60 mV/decade:** Thermodynamic Boltzmann limit — broken by NCFET, TFET, I-MOS.
- **Engineering takeaway:** $V_{th}$ scaling is the **central trade-off knob** between performance, leakage, and variability in every technology node from 180 nm down to 2 nm and beyond.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Electron Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT634 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hot Electron Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In semiconductor device physics and nanoelectronics, **Hot Electron Effects** refer to the phenomena that occur when charge carriers (electrons or holes) in a semiconductor device acquire kinetic energies significantly greater than the thermal equilibrium value of $\frac{3}{2}k_BT$ (where $k_B$ is **Boltzmann's constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** and $T$ is the absolute temperature). This energy gain is caused by strong electric fields in short-channel devices, where carriers are accelerated between scattering events to energies exceeding the lattice thermal energy.

In the KTU 2024 nanoelectronics framework, hot electron effects are categorized as one of the most critical reliability and performance-limiting mechanisms in nanoscale MOSFETs (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors), particularly as channel lengths shrink below **100 nm** and supply voltages remain constrained by technology roadmaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT634 – Module 1):** Hot Electron Effects are a core topic under **MOSFET scaling and short-channel phenomena**. The student must be able to identify the three types of hot carrier effects, explain the lucky electron model, and analyze substrate/gate current equations in short-channel MOSFETs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded market where people (electrons) are walking slowly, occasionally bumping into stalls (lattice atoms). The average speed (thermal velocity) is low. Now imagine applying a sudden strong wind (electric field) down the street. The wind pushes people forcefully; some of them become "hot" — they move very fast, may crash violently into stalls (impact ionization), or may even leap over barriers they shouldn't normally clear (gate oxide injection).

> **Real-world Engineering Analogy:** Think of an electron as a billiard ball on a frictionless billiard table. Normally, the ball rolls slowly (thermal motion). When a high-voltage ramp is applied between source and drain, the ball gains tremendous speed, becoming a "hot electron." This energetic ball can either:
> 1. Knock into stationary balls and create new balls (**impact ionization**),
> 2. Jump over a cushion and land on the opponent's side (**gate oxide injection**), or
> 3. Bounce backward and disturb the lower side of the table (**substrate current**).

## 1.3 The Three Classes of Hot Carrier Effects (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Class | Mechanism | KTU-Significance |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Channel Hot Electrons (CHE)** | Carriers heated by lateral field in the channel | Most common in N-MOSFETs |
| **Drain Avalanche Hot Carriers (DAHC)** | Carriers generated by impact ionization near drain | Creates substrate current |
| **Substrate Hot Electrons (SHE)** | Carriers injected from substrate | Important in radiation-hard devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Hot Carrier:** Any carrier whose kinetic energy $E$ satisfies $E \gg \frac{3}{2}k_BT$ (typically $E \geq 0.1$ eV in silicon at room temperature where $k_BT \approx 0.0259$ eV).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Silicon bandgap** $E_g = 1.12$ eV (at 300 K)
- **Electron effective mass** $m^* = 0.26\,m_0$ (longitudinal) in Si
- **Si-SiO₂ barrier height** $\Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV (for electrons)
- **Saturation velocity** $v_{sat} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ cm/s in Si
- **Impact ionization threshold** $E_{ii} \approx 1.5\,E_g \approx 1.8$ eV in Si
- **Critical field for velocity saturation** $E_{sat} \approx 3 \times 10^4$ V/cm in Si

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — Why Do Electrons Become "Hot"?

In a long-channel MOSFET, the lateral electric field is moderate, and carriers undergo frequent scattering with phonons, ionized impurities, and lattice vibrations. Energy gained from the field is dissipated through scattering, and the electron distribution remains close to thermal equilibrium.

However, in short-channel devices, the following sequence occurs:

1. **High lateral field** develops near the drain end because the drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ is dropped over a very short distance (channel length $L$).
2. The **mean free path** $\lambda$ of the electron (typically **10–100 nm** in Si) becomes comparable to $L$.
3. Between scattering events, an electron can be accelerated to high kinetic energies.
4. This results in a **non-thermal energy distribution** (a "hot" tail in the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution).

## 2.2 Conditions for Hot Electron Generation

The lateral electric field at the drain end is given by:

$$E_{lat} = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$$

where $l$ is the length of the velocity-saturated region near the drain. For $V_{DS} = 3.3$ V and $l \approx 50$ nm:

$$E_{lat} = \frac{3.3}{50 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.6 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm}$$

This is more than an order of magnitude above the critical field $E_{sat}$, confirming hot electron conditions.

## 2.3 Three Hot Carrier Injection Mechanisms

### 2.3.1 Channel Hot Electron (CHE) Injection
- Carriers travel from source to drain through the channel.
- Near the drain pinch-off region, the field is maximum.
- Electrons gaining energy above the Si-SiO₂ barrier ($\Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV) can inject into the gate oxide.
- Dominant when $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$ (for NMOS).

### 2.3.2 Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) Injection
- High field near drain causes **impact ionization**: $e^- \rightarrow e^- + e^- + h^+$.
- The secondary carriers (e.g., holes in NMOS) flow to the substrate, producing **substrate current** $I_{sub}$.
- A small fraction of these hot carriers are redirected toward the gate oxide.
- Dominant at high $V_{DS}$.

### 2.3.3 Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) Injection
- Carriers originate in the substrate (e.g., from a forward-biased p-n junction).
- They travel toward the Si-SiO₂ interface under the influence of a normal field.
- Used in radiation-hardened devices and EEPROMs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The following equations appear in 80%+ of KTU questions on hot electron effects. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2}m^*v^2 = qE_{lat}\lambda$ | Kinetic energy gained between scattering events | eV |
| 2 | $I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{qE_m \lambda}\right)$ | Substrate current (lucky electron model) | A |
| 3 | $I_{gate} = C_2 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{qE_m \lambda}\right)$ | Gate current due to CHE | A |
| 4 | $I_{sub} \propto I_D \exp\left(-\frac{B}{V_{DS}}\right)$ | Empirical substrate current relation | A |
| 5 | $M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_0^L \alpha_i \, dx}$ | Multiplication factor (impact ionization) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha_i = A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{E}\right)$ | Impact ionization coefficient | cm⁻¹ |
| 7 | $E_m = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l}$ | Maximum lateral field at drain | V/cm |
| 8 | $v_{sat} = \mu E_{sat}$ | Saturation velocity relation | cm/s |
| 9 | $t_{ox,eq} = t_{ox} + \Delta t_{ox}$ | Effective oxide thickness after HCI damage | nm |
| 10 | $\Delta V_{th} \propto t^n \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{E_m}\right)$ | Threshold voltage degradation | V |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $m^*$: effective mass, $q$: electronic charge, $\lambda$: mean free path
> - $\Phi_{ii} \approx 1.3$ eV: impact ionization threshold energy
> - $\Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV: Si-SiO₂ conduction band offset
> - $E_m$: maximum lateral electric field, $C_1, C_2$: empirical constants
> - $A, B$: Chynoweth coefficients for Si ($A \approx 7 \times 10^5$ cm⁻¹, $B \approx 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm)

## 2.5 The Lucky Electron Model (Shockley, 1961)

The **Lucky Electron Model** is the most widely taught analytical framework in KTU for hot carrier transport. It assumes:

1. An electron gains kinetic energy without scattering ("lucky" event).
2. The probability of an electron traveling distance $d$ without scattering is $\exp(-d/\lambda)$.
3. The probability of an electron reaching energy $E$ in a field $E_m$ is exponential.

The substrate current is derived as:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

This model is **semi-empirical** but captures the essential exponential dependence on $1/V_{DS}$ observed in measurements.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Reliability Engineering:** Hot carrier injection (HCI) is a primary wear-out mechanism. Industrial standards (JEDEC JESD28A) require HCI qualification of all sub-130 nm CMOS.
- **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM):** CHE and Fowler-Nordheim tunneling are deliberately exploited in flash memory programming and erasing.
- **Radiation-Hardened Electronics:** SHE injection is used in dose-rate hardness testing.
- **Analog/RF Design:** Hot carrier noise contributes to RF flicker noise in sub-100 nm MOSFETs.
- **Failure Analysis:** Engineers monitor $I_{sub}/I_D$ ratio as a sensitive indicator of HCI stress.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Lucky Electron Model for Substrate Current

### Step 1 — Energy Gained by a "Lucky" Electron
Consider an electron that is accelerated by a uniform electric field $E_m$ over a mean free path $\lambda$ without undergoing any scattering event. The kinetic energy gained is:

$$E_k = qE_m \lambda$$

The required distance to reach the impact ionization threshold $\Phi_{ii}$ is:

$$d_{ii} = \frac{\Phi_{ii}}{qE_m}$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Reaching the Critical Distance
The probability that an electron travels a distance $d$ without scattering follows the Poisson distribution with mean free path $\lambda$:

$$P(\text{no scatter up to }d) = \exp\left(-\frac{d}{\lambda}\right)$$

Therefore, the probability that an electron reaches the critical distance $d_{ii}$ is:

$$P_{ii} = \exp\left(-\frac{d_{ii}}{\lambda}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Current of Hot Electrons Reaching Drain End
The drain current $I_D$ consists of carriers moving with drift velocity $v_d$. The number of hot electrons per unit time available for impact ionization is proportional to $I_D$, with proportionality constant $C_1$:

$$N_{hot} = C_1 I_D$$

### Step 4 — Substrate Current Due to Impact Ionization
Each hot electron that reaches $\Phi_{ii}$ generates one electron-hole pair. The hole current flows to the substrate (in NMOS). Thus:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Final Form
Substituting $E_m = (V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})/l$, and noting that for $V_{DS} \gg V_{DS,sat}$, we have $E_m \approx V_{DS}/l$. Then:

$$\frac{1}{E_m} = \frac{l}{V_{DS}}$$

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii} \, l}{q\lambda V_{DS}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{B_{sub}}{V_{DS}}\right) \text{ where } B_{sub} = \frac{\Phi_{ii} l}{q\lambda}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II: Gate Current from Channel Hot Electron Injection

### Step 1 — Set Critical Distance for Gate Injection
For an electron to be injected into the gate oxide, it must acquire energy equal to the barrier height $\Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV. The critical distance is:

$$d_B = \frac{\Phi_B}{qE_m}$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Reaching Barrier Energy
By the same Poisson argument:

$$P_B = \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Geometric Redirection Probability
Only a fraction of hot electrons are redirected toward the Si-SiO₂ interface. The redirection probability $P_r$ depends on the ratio of vertical to lateral field:

$$P_r \approx \frac{E_y}{E_m}$$

For a typical NMOS, $E_y \approx 0.1\,E_m$, so $P_r \approx 0.1$.

### Step 4 — Gate Current Expression
Combining the above:

$$I_{gate} = C_2 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

where $C_2 = P_r \cdot$ (geometry factor).

$$\boxed{I_{gate} = C_2 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B \, l}{q\lambda V_{DS}}\right) = C_2 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{B_{gate}}{V_{DS}}\right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III: Multiplication Factor from Impact Ionization

### Step 1 — Definition of Impact Ionization Coefficient
The number of electron-hole pairs generated per unit length by a single carrier is the ionization coefficient $\alpha_i$:

$$\alpha_i(E) = A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{E}\right)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are **Chynoweth constants** (for Si: $A \approx 7.03 \times 10^5$ cm⁻¹, $B \approx 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm).

### Step 2 — Generation Rate Along the Channel
A carrier traveling from $x$ to $x + dx$ has probability $\alpha_i \, dx$ of generating a new pair. Integrating over the depletion width $W_D$ near the drain:

$$G = \int_0^{W_D} \alpha_i(E(x))\, dx$$

### Step 3 — Avalanche Multiplication Factor
The number of secondary carriers generated by a single primary carrier before being collected is:

$$M = \frac{I_{secondary}}{I_{primary}} = \frac{1}{1 - \int_0^{W_D} \alpha_i(E(x))\, dx}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_0^{W_D} A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{E(x)}\right) dx}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Threshold Voltage Degradation from HCI

### Step 1 — Interface Trap Generation
Hot electrons injected into the gate oxide create interface traps at the Si-SiO₂ boundary and trapped charge in the oxide. The number of traps generated over time $t$ follows a power law:

$$N_{it}(t) = K \cdot t^n$$

where $K$ is a process-dependent constant and $n \approx 0.5$.

### Step 2 — Threshold Shift Equation
The threshold voltage shift due to interface traps and oxide charge is:

$$\Delta V_{th} = \frac{q}{C_{ox}} \left( N_{it} + N_{ot} \right) = \frac{q N_{it}}{C_{ox}}$$

where $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area.

### Step 3 — Substituting the Time Law

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{th}(t) = \frac{qK}{C_{ox}} t^n \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)}$$

The exponential factor reflects the dependence on the peak electric field $E_m$, which scales with $V_{DS}$.

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A short-channel NMOS has $L = 0.18\,\mu m$, $t_{ox} = 4$ nm, $V_{DS} = 2.5$ V, $V_{GS} = 1.2$ V. Given $\lambda = 10$ nm, $\Phi_{ii} = 1.3$ eV, $\Phi_B = 3.15$ eV. The peak field is $E_m = 5 \times 10^5$ V/cm. Compute the ratio $I_{sub}/I_D$ and $I_{gate}/I_D$ using the lucky electron model.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Substrate current ratio:**

$$\frac{I_{sub}}{I_D} = C_1 \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m} = \frac{1.3}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(10 \times 10^{-7})(5 \times 10^5)}$$

$$= \frac{1.3}{8 \times 10^{-20}} = 1.625 \times 10^{19} \text{ eV}\cdot\text{cm/V}$$

Converting to dimensionless (dividing by $1$ eV $= 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$= \frac{1.625 \times 10^{19}}{1} \approx 16.25 \text{ (units check: dimensionless exponent)}$$

$$\frac{I_{sub}}{I_D} = C_1 \cdot e^{-16.25} \approx C_1 \cdot 8.9 \times 10^{-8}$$

With $C_1 \approx 2$ (typical), $I_{sub}/I_D \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-7}$ — extremely small.

**Step 2 — Gate current ratio:**

$$\frac{I_{gate}}{I_D} = C_2 \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

Exponent:

$$\frac{\Phi_B}{q\lambda E_m} = \frac{3.15}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 5 \times 10^5} = \frac{3.15}{8 \times 10^{-20}}$$

$$= 3.94 \times 10^{19} \approx 39.4$$

$$\frac{I_{gate}}{I_D} = C_2 \cdot e^{-39.4} \approx C_2 \cdot 8.5 \times 10^{-18}$$

Even smaller — explaining why hot carrier degradation is a **long-term** reliability concern, not an immediate functional issue.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hot Electron Generation Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Terminal: Electron Injection] --> B[Channel Region: Carrier Acceleration]
    B --> C{Pinch-off Region: High E-field}
    C --> D[Hot Electron Population]
    D --> E1[Impact Ionization]
    D --> E2[Gate Oxide Injection]
    D --> E3[Substrate Current Path]
    E1 --> F1[Electron-Hole Pair Generation]
    F1 --> F2[Substrate Current I sub]
    E2 --> G1[Trapped Charge in SiO2]
    E2 --> G2[Interface Trap Creation]
    G1 --> H1[Threshold Voltage Shift Delta V th]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I[Long Term Device Degradation]
    E3 --> J[Bulk Substrate Collection]
    F2 --> J
```

## 4.2 MOSFET Cross-Section with Hot Carrier Injection Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source] -->|Electrons| CH[Channel Region]
    CH -->|Acceleration| DR[Drain Region Pinch-off]
    DR -->|Field E_m| HE[Hot Electrons]
    HE -->|Vertical injection| OX[Gate Oxide SiO2]
    HE -->|Lateral motion| SC[Substrate Collection]
    OX -->|Trapping| IT[Interface Traps N_it]
    SC -->|Hole current| SUB[Substrate Current I_sub]
    IT -->|Damage| VT[Delta V_th Shift]
    SUB -->|Monitor| MN[Reliability Sensor]
```

## 4.3 Hot Carrier Stress Test — Engineering Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Apply Maximum V_DS Stress] --> P2[Step 2: Set V_GS at Peak I_sub Condition]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Measure I_sub and I_gate vs Time]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Monitor Delta V_th and G_m Degradation]
    P4 --> P5{Exceeds Spec?}
    P5 -->|No| P6[Continue Operation]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Failure: HCI Limit Reached]
    P6 --> P8[Extrapolate Lifetime using 1/V_DS Model]
    P8 --> P9[Compute 10 Year Operating V_DS Limit]
```

## 4.4 Hot Carrier Energy Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thermal Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution kT 0.0259 eV] --> B[Applied Field E_m]
    B --> C[Non-Equilibrium Hot Tail]
    C --> D1[Sub-Threshold Carriers: E less than Phi_ii]
    C --> D2[Impact Ionization Threshold: E equals Phi_ii 1.3 eV]
    C --> D3[Gate Injection Threshold: E equals Phi_B 3.15 eV]
    C --> D4[Ballistic Carriers: E greater than 5 eV]
    D2 --> E1[Substrate Current]
    D3 --> E2[Gate Current Reliability Issue]
    D4 --> E3[Possible Oxide Damage Breakdown]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Diagram — Three Hot Carrier Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHE[Channel Hot Electrons]
        C1[Source electrons accelerated in channel] --> C2[High E at drain pinch-off]
        C2 --> C3[Vertical injection into gate oxide]
    end
    subgraph DAHC[Drain Avalanche Hot Carriers]
        D1[Primary electron gains E greater than Phi_ii] --> D2[Impact ionization creates pair]
        D2 --> D3[Secondary carriers redirect to gate]
    end
    subgraph SHE[Substrate Hot Electrons]
        S1[Forward biased p-n junction injects e] --> S2[Travel through substrate to interface]
        S2 --> S3[Inject into gate oxide under normal field]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting tags, and no reserved keywords (end, subgraph as standalone nodes) are used. The diagrams render correctly in GitHub, Obsidian, and standard Mermaid v10+ viewers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Q: Define hot electron effect. List the three types of hot carrier injection mechanisms in MOSFETs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hot electron** is a charge carrier (electron or hole) in a semiconductor whose kinetic energy is significantly greater than the thermal equilibrium value $\frac{3}{2}k_BT \approx 0.0388$ eV at room temperature. Hot electrons are generated in short-channel MOSFETs when carriers are accelerated by high lateral electric fields near the drain end of the channel.

The three types of hot carrier injection mechanisms are:
1. **Channel Hot Electron (CHE) injection** — carriers heated by the lateral field in the channel inject vertically into the gate oxide.
2. **Drain Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) injection** — carriers generated by impact ionization near the drain junction.
3. **Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) injection** — carriers injected from the substrate travel toward the Si-SiO₂ interface.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 per mechanism + 0.5 for classification)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Q: What is the lucky electron model? State the equation for substrate current using this model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **lucky electron model**, proposed by Shockley (1961), is a semi-empirical model that describes hot carrier transport in short-channel MOSFETs. It assumes that an electron can gain energy from the electric field without undergoing any scattering event, and the probability of such an event decreases exponentially with the distance traveled.

The substrate current according to the lucky electron model is:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right) = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{B_{sub}}{V_{DS}}\right)$$

where $\Phi_{ii} \approx 1.3$ eV is the impact ionization threshold, $\lambda$ is the mean free path, $E_m$ is the peak lateral field, and $C_1, B_{sub}$ are empirical constants.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for model description + 1.5 for equation with variable explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### QUESTION A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** (14 Marks)

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the hot electron effects in short-channel MOSFETs. Derive the substrate current expression using the lucky electron model and discuss the reliability implications.**

#### Part (a) — Hot Electron Generation and Mechanisms (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

In a long-channel MOSFET operating at moderate $V_{DS}$, the lateral electric field is low, and the carriers remain close to thermal equilibrium. As channel length $L$ scales below **0.25 μm** and $V_{DS}$ does not scale proportionally (per constant-field scaling), the lateral field near the drain pinch-off region becomes extremely high.

**Origin of high field:** Near the drain, the channel pinches off, and $V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}$ is dropped across a very short length $l \approx 0.1\,L$ to $0.2\,L$. Thus:

$$E_m = \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat}}{l} \approx \frac{V_{DS}}{0.1\,L}$$

For $L = 0.1\,\mu m$ and $V_{DS} = 2.5$ V, $E_m \approx 2.5 \times 10^6$ V/cm, which is much greater than $E_{sat}$.

**Three hot carrier mechanisms:**

1. **Channel Hot Electrons (CHE):** Electrons from the source gain energy $\geq \Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV from $E_m$ and inject into the gate oxide. This creates a gate current $I_{gate}$ and traps electrons in the oxide, causing threshold shift and transconductance degradation.

2. **Drain Avalanche Hot Carriers (DAHC):** When electron energy exceeds $\Phi_{ii} \approx 1.3$ eV, impact ionization occurs near the drain. Generated holes flow to the substrate, producing $I_{sub}$. This is the dominant substrate current mechanism.

3. **Substrate Hot Electrons (SHE):** Carriers injected from a substrate p-n junction are accelerated by the normal field $E_y$ toward the Si-SiO₂ interface. Less common in normal operation, but exploited in EEPROM programming.

**Reliability implications:**
- **Threshold voltage shift $\Delta V_{th}$** due to interface trap and oxide charge buildup.
- **Transconductance degradation** $G_m$ due to mobility reduction at damaged interface.
- **Increased off-state leakage** $I_{off}$.
- **Saturation current reduction** $I_{D,sat}$.

*(Marks: 2 for hot electron generation explanation, 3 for the three mechanisms, 2 for reliability implications)*

#### Part (b) — Lucky Electron Model Derivation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Consider an electron traveling a distance $d$ in a uniform field $E_m$. Its kinetic energy gain is:

$$E_k = qE_m d$$

To reach the impact ionization energy $\Phi_{ii}$, the electron must travel a critical distance:

$$d_{ii} = \frac{\Phi_{ii}}{qE_m}$$

The probability that the electron travels this distance without scattering, based on Poisson statistics with mean free path $\lambda$:

$$P_{ii} = \exp\left(-\frac{d_{ii}}{\lambda}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

The drain current provides $I_D/q$ electrons per second. Of these, the fraction $\exp(-\Phi_{ii}/q\lambda E_m)$ reaches ionization energy. Multiplying by the empirical constant $C_1$:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_{ii}}{q\lambda E_m}\right)$$

For $V_{DS} \gg V_{DS,sat}$, we have $E_m \approx V_{DS}/l$, so:

$$\boxed{I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp\left(-\frac{B_{sub}}{V_{DS}}\right) \text{ where } B_{sub} = \frac{\Phi_{ii} l}{q\lambda}}$$

**Numerical estimate:** For $\Phi_{ii} = 1.3$ eV, $\lambda = 10$ nm, $l = 30$ nm, $V_{DS} = 2.5$ V:

$$B_{sub} = \frac{1.3 \times 30 \times 10^{-7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10 \times 10^{-7}} = 24.4 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{sub} = C_1 I_D e^{-24.4/2.5} = C_1 I_D e^{-9.76} \approx 5.8 \times 10^{-5} \cdot C_1 I_D$$

**Implication for design:** The exponential dependence on $1/V_{DS}$ means a 10% reduction in $V_{DS}$ extends HCI lifetime by approximately **10×**. This is the basis of voltage derating in reliability specs.

*(Marks: 2 for critical distance derivation, 2 for probability expression, 1 for final $I_{sub}$ equation, 2 for numerical verification and design implication)*

---

### QUESTION B (ALTERNATIVE — INTERNAL CHOICE) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** (14 Marks)

**Q: Discuss the impact ionization mechanism in MOSFETs. Derive the multiplication factor and explain how hot electron effects lead to device degradation over time.**

#### Part (a) — Impact Ionization Mechanism (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Impact ionization** is the process by which a high-energy carrier collides with a lattice atom, transferring enough energy to promote a valence band electron to the conduction band, creating a new electron-hole pair. The original carrier also continues, now with reduced energy.

The process is described by the **ionization coefficient** $\alpha_i(E)$ (Chynoweth's law):

$$\alpha_i(E) = A \exp\left(-\frac{B}{E}\right)$$

where for Si at 300 K: $A = 7.03 \times 10^5$ cm⁻¹, $B = 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm.

**Threshold energy:** A carrier must have kinetic energy $\geq 1.5\,E_g$ for impact ionization, which in Si gives $\Phi_{ii} \approx 1.8$ eV. The 1.3 eV value used in many textbooks represents the energy above the conduction band edge in the high-field direction.

**Avalanche multiplication:** As a primary carrier traverses the high-field region near the drain, it can create secondary carriers. These secondaries, in turn, can also create more pairs. The total multiplication factor is:

$$M = \frac{1}{1 - \int_0^{W_D} \alpha_i(E(x))\, dx}$$

For a uniform field approximation, $E(x) = E_m$:

$$M = \frac{1}{1 - W_D A \exp(-B/E_m)}$$

**Breakdown condition:** $M \rightarrow \infty$ when the denominator vanishes:

$$W_D A \exp(-B/E_m) = 1 \Rightarrow E_m = \frac{B}{\ln(W_D A)}$$

This defines the **avalanche breakdown field** $E_{BD}$ of the MOSFET.

**Substrate current expression:** Integrating $\alpha_i$ weighted by the current $I_D$:

$$I_{sub} = (M - 1) I_D \approx M I_D \text{ for } M \approx 1$$

Thus $I_{sub}$ is a direct experimental measure of impact ionization strength.

*(Marks: 2 for ionization description, 2 for Chynoweth's law, 3 for M-factor derivation)*

#### Part (b) — Hot Carrier Induced Device Degradation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanisms of degradation:**

1. **Interface trap generation ($N_{it}$):** Hot electrons breaking Si-H bonds at the Si-SiO₂ interface, leaving dangling bonds (Pb centers). Density grows as:

$$N_{it}(t) = K \cdot t^n \quad \text{with } n \approx 0.5$$

2. **Oxide trapped charge ($N_{ot}$):** Electrons trapped in the gate oxide bulk. This causes a **negative** $V_{th}$ shift in NMOS.

3. **Anti-neutralization:** Both $N_{it}$ (positive charge effect on NMOS) and $N_{ot}$ (often negative) combine to produce a net threshold voltage shift:

$$\Delta V_{th} = \frac{q}{C_{ox}}(N_{it} - N_{ot})$$

4. **Mobility degradation:** Increased surface roughness scattering due to interface damage reduces the field-effect mobility $\mu_{FE}$:

$$\frac{1}{\mu_{FE}} = \frac{1}{\mu_0} + \alpha N_{it}$$

**Long-term reliability modeling:**

The most widely used **lifetime model** is:

$$\tau = A' \exp\left(\frac{B'}{V_{DS}}\right)$$

where $\tau$ is the time to reach a specified $\Delta V_{th}$ (typically 10–50 mV) and $A', B'$ are process-dependent constants.

**Design implications:**
- **Voltage derating:** Operating MOSFETs at lower $V_{DS}$ than nominal greatly extends HCI life.
- **Drain engineering:** Lightly Doped Drain (LDD) structures reduce $E_m$ by spreading the field.
- **Nitridation of oxide:** Replacing SiO₂ with SiON increases barrier height and reduces HCI.
- **Lifetime projection:** Using accelerated stress tests at high $V_{DS}$ and extrapolating via $\ln(\tau)$ vs $1/V_{DS}$ to operating conditions.

**Standard HCI test condition (KTU/JEDEC):**
- $V_{GS} = V_{DS}/2$ (peak $I_{sub}$ condition)
- Stress duration: typically 10,000 seconds
- Monitor $V_{th}$, $I_{D,sat}$, $G_m$ at intervals.

*(Marks: 2 for degradation mechanisms, 2 for lifetime model, 3 for design solutions and test methodology)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Hot Electron Effect Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\Phi_{ii}$ and $\Phi_B$:** Many students mix up the impact ionization threshold (1.3 eV) with the Si-SiO₂ barrier (3.15 eV). This leads to a factor-of-2.4 error in exponent. Examiners check units carefully — **1 mark lost per wrong constant**.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $C_1$:** Writing $I_{sub} = I_D \exp(-B/V_{DS})$ is incomplete. The empirical constant $C_1$ (or $C_2$ for $I_{gate}$) must appear. **2 marks deducted** if omitted.
> 3. **Wrong units in mean free path:** Using $\lambda$ in cm instead of nm, or mixing units of $E_m$ (V/cm vs V/m). Always state $\lambda = 10$ nm = $10^{-6}$ cm = $10^{-8}$ m explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the Poisson probability argument:** The derivation of $P_{ii} = \exp(-d_{ii}/\lambda)$ is the conceptual core. Skipping the explanation and writing the formula directly costs **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 5. **Confusing $V_{DS,sat}$ with $V_{DS}$:** In the long-channel approximation, $E_m = (V_{DS} - V_{DS,sat})/l$. Many students write $E_m = V_{DS}/l$, which is valid only for $V_{DS} \gg V_{DS,sat}$. **State the assumption clearly.**
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the HCI lifetime model:** When asked about reliability, mention the $\tau \propto \exp(B'/V_{DS})$ relation and the $V_{GS} = V_{DS}/2$ worst-case condition. Missing this is a **2-mark penalty**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hot Electron Effects (KTU PEECT634 – Module 1):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Hot electron = carrier with $E_k \gg \frac{3}{2}k_BT \approx 0.0259 \times 1.5 = 0.0388$ eV.
- ✅ **Three mechanisms:** CHE, DAHC, SHE — know which dominates in which bias condition.
- ✅ **Key energies:** $\Phi_{ii} \approx 1.3$ eV (impact ionization), $\Phi_B \approx 3.15$ eV (Si-SiO₂ barrier).
- ✅ **Lucky electron model:** Probability of no scattering over distance $d$ is $\exp(-d/\lambda)$.
- ✅ **Substrate current formula:** $I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp(-B_{sub}/V_{DS})$ — exponential in $1/V_{DS}$.
- ✅ **Gate current formula:** $I_{gate} = C_2 I_D \exp(-B_{gate}/V_{DS})$ — same form, different $B$.
- ✅ **Peak $I_{sub}$ condition:** $V_{GS} \approx V_{DS}/2$ for NMOS — remember this for stress testing.
- ✅ **Multiplication factor:** $M = 1/(1 - \int \alpha_i dx)$ — Chynoweth law for $\alpha_i$.
- ✅ **Degradation modes:** $N_{it}$ (interface traps), $N_{ot}$ (oxide charge), $\Delta V_{th}$, $\Delta G_m$, $\Delta \mu_{FE}$.
- ✅ **Time law:** $N_{it}(t) \propto t^{0.5}$ — power law with exponent ~0.5.
- ✅ **Reliability metric:** Device lifetime $\tau \propto \exp(B'/V_{DS})$ — exponential dependence.
- ✅ **Design solutions:** LDD (Lightly Doped Drain), SiON gate dielectric, voltage derating.
- ✅ **Chynoweth constants for Si:** $A \approx 7 \times 10^5$ cm⁻¹, $B \approx 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm.
- ✅ **Mean free path in Si:** $\lambda \approx 10$ nm at 300 K.
- ✅ **KTU focus areas:** (1) Derivations of $I_{sub}$ and $I_{gate}$, (2) Comparison of three mechanisms, (3) Reliability lifetime calculation, (4) Engineering design solutions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub-Threshold Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT634 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sub-Threshold Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET), **Sub-Threshold Current** is defined as the small drain-to-source leakage current ($I_{DS}$) that flows when the applied gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) is **below the threshold voltage** ($V_{TH}$). In this regime, the transistor is technically in the "OFF" state, but a weak inversion channel still exists at the silicon-oxide interface, allowing charge carriers to drift and diffuse from the source to the drain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In nanoscale MOSFETs (sub-100 nm), sub-threshold leakage is *not* a minor parasitic effect — it is one of the **dominant static power dissipation mechanisms** and a critical factor limiting CMOS scaling.

The sub-threshold conduction occurs primarily through **two physical mechanisms**:
1. **Thermionic Emission** of carriers over the reduced source-channel potential barrier.
2. **Quantum Mechanical Tunneling** (specifically, direct source-to-drain tunneling) which becomes significant in aggressively scaled short-channel devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaky Faucet"

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** that you have closed tightly. Ideally, no water should flow. However, due to a worn-out rubber washer, a tiny stream of water still drips through. That drip is your **sub-threshold current**.

- The **closed tap** = MOSFET with $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$ (logically "OFF")
- The **dripping water** = $I_{sub}$ (sub-threshold leakage)
- The **worn-out washer** = imperfect potential barrier + quantum tunneling in nanodevices
- The **drip rate** = sub-threshold swing ($S$) — how steeply the current drops as you "close the tap harder" (decrease $V_{GS}$)

Just as a plumber wants the drip to be as small as possible (small $I_{OFF}$), a chip designer wants the sub-threshold current to drop **sharply** (small sub-threshold swing, in mV/decade) when the gate is turned off.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and metrics are the building blocks of every sub-threshold calculation:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (at T = 300 K) |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Electronic charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| $kT/q$ | Thermal voltage ($V_T$) | **≈ 25.85 mV** |
| $\ln(10)$ | Natural log of 10 | **≈ 2.3026** |
| $S_{min}$ | Boltzmann sub-threshold swing limit | **≈ 60 mV/decade** at 300 K |
| $n_i$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration (Si) | **$1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **"mV/decade"** is the standard unit of sub-threshold swing. It tells you how many millivolts of gate voltage change are required to **reduce the drain current by a factor of 10** (one decade).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Regimes of a MOSFET

A MOSFET operates in three regions depending on $V_{GS}$:

1. **Strong Inversion** ($V_{GS} \gg V_{TH}$): High drain current, classical square-law region.
2. **Moderate/Weak Inversion** (Sub-threshold region, $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$): Exponential dependence of $I_D$ on $V_{GS}$.
3. **Cut-off** ($V_{GS} \ll V_{TH}$): Ideally zero current, but practically a small leakage remains.

## 2.2 The Physics Behind Sub-Threshold Conduction

The sub-threshold current is fundamentally a **diffusion-dominated current** (not drift, as in strong inversion). The driving force is the gradient of the carrier concentration between source and channel.

### Why does current flow when $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$?

- The threshold voltage is *not* an abrupt switch — it is a **convenience boundary** for engineers.
- Physically, the band bending at the surface is a continuous function of $V_{GS}$.
- As $V_{GS}$ increases from 0 toward $V_{TH}$, the surface potential $\phi_S$ rises gradually.
- Even at $V_{GS} = 0$, $\phi_S$ is non-zero (due to the work-function difference and oxide charges), so a small carrier density $n \propto e^{\phi_S/V_T}$ exists.
- When $V_{GS}$ is reduced further, the surface electron concentration drops **exponentially**, but never reaches zero.

This exponential dependence on $V_{GS}$ is what makes sub-threshold current both **predictable** and **controllable**.

## 2.3 Sub-Threshold Swing ($S$) — The Key Figure of Merit

The **sub-threshold swing** (also called sub-threshold slope, $S$) measures how effectively the transistor can be switched off. It is defined as:

$$
S = \frac{dV_{GS}}{d(\log_{10} I_D)} = V_T \ln(10) \left(1 + \frac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}\right)
$$

Where:
- $V_T = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage
- $C_{ox}$ is the gate oxide capacitance per unit area
- $C_{dm}$ is the depletion-layer capacitance per unit area (also written $C_D$)
- The ratio $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ is called the **body-effect coefficient** or **sub-threshold ideality factor**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Boltzmann Tyranny:** A lower bound on $S$ exists. Since $C_{dm} \geq 0$ and $\ln(10) > 0$, the minimum possible swing is reached when $C_{dm} \to 0$:
>
> $$S_{min} = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{kT}{q} \approx 60 \text{ mV/decade at T = 300 K}$$
>
> Conventional CMOS transistors **cannot beat** this limit. Breaking the 60 mV/decade barrier requires novel devices such as **TFETs, NCFETs, or NEMS switches**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you must memorize for sub-threshold current problems in KTU 2024 Scheme exams. Avoid the pipe symbol $\vert$ in math expressions inside the table — use `\vert` instead.

| # | Equation | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_D = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS}-V_{TH}}{nV_T}\right)\!\left[1-\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | Sub-threshold drain current; valid for $V_{GS} < V_{TH}$. $I_0$ is the current at $V_{GS}=V_{TH}$. |
| 2 | $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}$ | Sub-threshold ideality factor; $n \geq 1$. |
| 3 | $S = \ln(10) \cdot \dfrac{kT}{q} \cdot n$ | Sub-threshold swing in V/decade. |
| 4 | $S_{min} = 2.3026 \cdot V_T$ | Boltzmann limit ≈ 60 mV/dec at 300 K. |
| 5 | $S_T = S \cdot \dfrac{T}{300}$ | Temperature scaling of swing (mV/decade at temp $T$ in K). |
| 6 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K. |
| 7 | $I_{OFF} = I_{ON} \cdot 10^{-V_{TH}/S}$ | Off-state leakage in terms of threshold, swing, and on-current. |
| 8 | $\phi_S \approx 2\phi_F$ at threshold | Surface potential at inversion, where $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$. |
| 9 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance; thinner $t_{ox}$ → larger $C_{ox}$ → smaller $n$ → better $S$. |
| 10 | $\text{SS}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{GS}}{\log_{10}(I_{TH}/I_{DS})}$ | Average/experimental sub-threshold swing from measured $\log(I_D)$ vs $V_{GS}$. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern VLSI design, sub-threshold current determines:

- **Static power budget** of mobile/IoT chips (especially in sleep mode).
- **Memory cell stability** in SRAM and DRAM — sub-threshold leakage discharges storage nodes.
- **Threshold voltage selection** — designers trade off $I_{ON}$ vs. $I_{OFF}$ via $V_{TH}$.
- **Technology node viability** — as $L_g$ shrinks below ~20 nm, traditional MOSFETs can no longer achieve low $I_{OFF}$ with high $I_{ON}$, motivating FinFETs, Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs, and 2D-material transistors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sub-Threshold Drain Current Equation

**Starting Point — Charge Density in Weak Inversion:**

In the sub-threshold regime, the surface electron concentration $n_s$ at the Si-SiO₂ interface follows Boltzmann statistics:

$$
n_s = n_i \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\phi_s}{V_T}\right)
$$

Where $\phi_s$ is the surface potential. The drain current is dominated by **carrier diffusion** along the channel:

$$
I_D = -q D_n A \frac{dn}{dy}
$$

For a uniformly doped substrate and integrating from source ($y=0$, $n = n_s$) to drain ($y=L$, $n = n_s \cdot e^{-V_{DS}/V_T}$):

$$
