$$ = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 200\cdot 20\cdot 2.25 = 4500\,\mu A = 4.5\,mA $$

**Step C:** Apply CLM correction:
$$ I_D = 4.5\,mA \cdot (1 + 0.04 \cdot 5) = 4.5\,mA \cdot 1.2 = \boxed{5.4\,mA} $$

**Step D:** Output conductance:
$$ g_{ds} = \lambda I_D = 0.04 \cdot 5.4\,mA = \boxed{0.216\,mS} $$

**Step E:** Output resistance:
$$ r_o = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{0.216 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{4629.6\,\Omega \approx 4.63\,k\Omega} $$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #2 — EOT Calculation

**Problem:** A 45 nm gate stack consists of: (i) 0.7 nm SiO₂ interface layer (κ = 3.9), (ii) 2.0 nm HfO₂ (κ = 22). Calculate (a) total physical thickness, (b) EOT of each layer, (c) total EOT, (d) equivalent capacitance per unit area.

### Solution:
**(a) Total physical thickness:**
$$ t_{phys,total} = 0.7 + 2.0 = \boxed{2.7\,nm} $$

**(b) EOT of each layer:**
$$ EOT_{SiO_2} = \frac{3.9}{3.9}\cdot 0.7 = \boxed{0.7\,nm} $$
$$ EOT_{HfO_2} = \frac{3.9}{22}\cdot 2.0 = 0.3545 = \boxed{0.355\,nm} $$

**(c) Total EOT (series):**
$$ EOT_{total} = 0.7 + 0.355 = \boxed{1.055\,nm} $$

**(d) Capacitance per unit area:**
$$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}\varepsilon_0}{EOT_{total}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854\times 10^{-12}}{1.055\times 10^{-9}} = \boxed{32.7\,mF/m^2 = 3.27\,\mu F/cm^2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison to pure SiO₂:** A pure 1.055 nm SiO₂ layer would have the SAME C_ox but ~10⁵–10⁶ times higher leakage current. The 2.7 nm high-K stack provides the same capacitance with vastly reduced tunneling — the entire motivation for high-K integration.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Equations

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Variables | Physical Meaning |
|------------|--------------------|-----------|------------------|
| CLM I_D | I_D = ½μC_ox(W/L)(V_GS−V_th)²(1+λV_DS) | λ, V_DS | Current increases linearly with V_DS in SAT |
| Effective Length | L' = L − ΔL | ΔL | Channel shortens with V_DS |
| λ scaling | λ ≈ (1/L)·√(2ε_si·ε_0/qN_A) | L, N_A | Shorter L → larger λ → worse CLM |
| Output Resistance | r_o = 1/(λI_D) | λ, I_D | Finite output resistance limits gain |
| EOT (single layer) | EOT = (3.9/κ)·t_phys | κ, t_phys | Electrical thickness referenced to SiO₂ |
| EOT (multi-layer) | EOT_total = Σ EOT_i | layer stack | Series capacitance additivity |
| Gate Leakage | J_gate ∝ exp(−t_phys/λ_T) | t_phys | Thicker high-K → lower leakage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Channel Length Modulation — Physical Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Source\nV=0"] --> nodeB["Channel Region\nL effective"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Pinch-off Point\nV = V_DS sat"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Depletion Region\nDelta L width"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Drain Contact\nV = V_DS"]
    nodeF["Gate Oxide"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeG["Gate Electrode\nV = V_GS"] -.-> nodeF

    nodeH["V_DS increases"] --> nodeI["Depletion widens"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Delta L increases"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Effective L decreases"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["I_D increases beyond saturation"]
```

**Interpretation:** As V_DS rises beyond V_DS,sat, the depletion region at the drain encroaches into the channel by ΔL. The effective channel shortens from L to (L − ΔL), forcing more current to flow.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: I_D–V_DS Characteristics With and Without CLM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["V_DS axis"] --> nodeB["Triode Region\nV_DS less than V_DS sat"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Knee Point\nV_DS equals V_GS minus V_th"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Ideal Saturation\nHorizontal line I_D constant"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["With CLM\nSloped line I_D rises linearly"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Extrapolation meets V_DS axis\nat V_DS equals minus V_A"]

    nodeG["Slope is lambda I_D"] -.-> nodeE
    nodeH["V_A equals 1 over lambda"] -.-> nodeF
```

**Interpretation:** The I_D–V_DS curve is flat without CLM. With CLM, it slopes upward in saturation. Extrapolating the sloped line back to the V_DS axis gives −V_A (the Early voltage).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Modern High-K Metal Gate Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Metal Gate\nTiN or TaN"] --> nodeB["High-K Layer\nHfO2 or HfSiON\nthickness 2 to 3 nm"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Interface Layer\nSiO2 or SiON\nthickness 0.5 to 0.7 nm"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Si Substrate\nChannel Region"]
    nodeE["Poly Si Cap"] -.-> nodeA

    nodeF["Series Capacitance Model"] --> nodeG["C metal"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["C high-K"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["C interface layer"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["1 over C total equals\n1 over C metal plus 1 over C high-K plus 1 over C IL"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["EOT total equals EOT metal plus EOT high-K plus EOT IL"]
```

**Interpretation:** Real CMOS gate stacks are multilayer. The total EOT is the sum of individual layer EOTs (series-capacitance additivity).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Comparison — SiO₂ Scaling Limit vs High-K Breakthrough

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Aggressive Scaling\nt ox less than 1.5 nm"] --> nodeB["Direct Tunneling\nJ gate exceeds 1 A per cm squared"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Unsuitable for Low Power Logic"]

    nodeD["High-K Dielectric\nkappa greater than 20"] --> nodeE["Physical Thickness\n5 to 10 nm\nsame EOT"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Tunneling Reduced\nby factor 100 to 1000"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Same C ox\nLow Leakage\nSuitable for Sub 45 nm"]

    nodeH["Industry Adoption\nIntel 45 nm Node 2007"] -.-> nodeG
```

**Interpretation:** The high-K breakthrough preserves capacitance (small EOT) while physically thickening the insulator (large t_phys), suppressing tunneling leakage by 2–3 orders of magnitude.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Block-Level Functional Flow — EOT Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input: Material Selection\nHigh-K candidate\nExample HfO2"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Obtain Permittivity\nkappa equals 22"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Specify Physical Thickness\nt phys equals 2.5 nm"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Apply Formula\nEOT equals 3.9 divided by kappa times t phys"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Compute EOT\nequal to 0.443 nm"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 5: Compute C ox\nC ox equals kappa SiO2 epsilon 0 divided by EOT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 6: Evaluate Leakage\nJ gate proportional to exp minus t phys by lambda T"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Output: Design Decision\nEOT target met and Leakage acceptable"]

    nodeI["Add Interface Layer SiO2\n0.7 nm"] --> nodeJ["EOT total equals EOT IL plus EOT high-K\nequal to 1.143 nm"]
    nodeJ --> nodeH
```

**Interpretation:** EOT calculation is a multi-step design verification flow — from material choice → κ → t_phys → EOT → C_ox → leakage check → design acceptance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define channel length modulation in a MOSFET and write the modified saturation drain current equation including the parameter λ.**

**Model Answer:**
Channel length modulation is the phenomenon in which the effective electrical length of the MOSFET inversion channel is reduced as the drain-to-source voltage increases beyond the saturation voltage (V_DS,sat = V_GS − V_th). This occurs due to the lateral expansion of the reverse-biased drain-body p-n junction depletion region into the channel. The parameter λ (channel length modulation parameter) quantifies this effect.

$$ I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS}) $$

The output resistance is $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ and the Early voltage is $V_A = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Define Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT). For a HfO₂ layer of 5 nm physical thickness with κ = 22, calculate the EOT.**

**Model Answer:**
Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT) is defined as the thickness of an equivalent SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual high-K dielectric stack.

$$ \text{EOT} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high-K}} \cdot t_{phys} $$

For HfO₂:
$$ \text{EOT} = \frac{3.9}{22} \cdot 5\,nm = 0.886\,nm $$

The 5 nm HfO₂ layer is electrically equivalent to **0.886 nm of SiO₂** but provides much lower tunneling leakage due to its larger physical thickness. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the modified saturation drain current equation for an n-channel MOSFET including the channel length modulation parameter λ. Explain how λ depends on the effective channel length and substrate doping concentration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A NMOS device has the following parameters: W/L = 50, μ_n C_ox = 250 μA/V², V_th = 0.6 V, λ = 0.05 V⁻¹. Calculate the drain current I_D, output conductance g_ds, and output resistance r_o when V_GS = 3 V and V_DS = 4 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1:** State the classical saturation equation without CLM:
$$ I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 $$

**Step 2:** When V_DS > V_DS,sat, the drain depletion region widens by ΔL into the channel. The effective length becomes L' = L − ΔL. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** The current using L' is:
$$ I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L-\Delta L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 $$

**Step 4:** Taylor expand 1/(L − ΔL) ≈ (1/L)(1 + ΔL/L) for ΔL ≪ L. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Define λ via the empirical relation ΔL/L ≈ λV_DS. Substituting:
$$ I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS}) $$

**Step 6:** Derive λ from junction physics. For one-sided abrupt junction:
$$ \Delta L = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_{si}\varepsilon_0(V_{DS}-V_{DS,sat})}{qN_A}} $$

For ΔL ≪ L: $\lambda \approx \dfrac{1}{L}\sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}\varepsilon_0}{qN_A}}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Conclude — λ decreases with increasing L and increases with decreasing N_A. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1:** Compute V_DS,sat = V_GS − V_th = 3 − 0.6 = **2.4 V**. Since V_DS = 4 V > 2.4 V, the device is in **saturation**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Compute I_D using modified equation:
$$ I_D = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 250\,\mu A/V^2 \cdot 50 \cdot (3-0.6)^2 \cdot (1 + 0.05 \cdot 4) $$
$$ I_D = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 250 \cdot 50 \cdot 5.76 \cdot 1.2 $$
$$ I_D = 125\cdot 50 \cdot 5.76 \cdot 1.2 = 43{,}200\,\mu A = \boxed{43.2\,mA} $$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 3:** Output conductance:
$$ g_{ds} = \lambda I_D = 0.05 \cdot 43.2\,mA = \boxed{2.16\,mS} $$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Output resistance:
$$ r_o = \frac{1}{g_{ds}} = \frac{1}{2.16 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{462.96\,\Omega} $$ **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the physical mechanism of channel length modulation in a short-channel MOSFET. Discuss its impact on output resistance and the intrinsic voltage gain of a common-source amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of Effective Oxide Thickness (EOT) in high-K gate dielectrics. A gate stack consists of a 0.6 nm SiO₂ interface layer and a 2.5 nm HfO₂ (κ = 22) layer. Calculate the total EOT, the equivalent C_ox, and compare the gate leakage reduction vs. an equivalent 0.96 nm pure SiO₂ gate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Step 1:** Draw the MOSFET cross-section showing gate, oxide, source, drain, and channel. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Explain that as V_DS > V_DS,sat, the depletion region around the reverse-biased drain-body junction expands laterally into the channel by ΔL. The effective channel becomes L' = L − ΔL. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Show that the I_D–V_DS curve is no longer flat in saturation but has a positive slope; the slope is λ·I_D. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Output resistance r_o = 1/(λI_D). Since λ ∝ 1/L, shorter-channel devices have lower r_o. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Intrinsic gain of common-source amplifier: A_v = g_m·r_o. With CLM:
$$ A_v = g_m \cdot r_o = \frac{2(V_{GS}-V_{th})}{V_{DS,sat}(1+\lambda V_{DS})}\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda} \text{ (approximately)} $$

CLM degrades the gain by factor (1 + λV_DS); designers use **cascode** topology to mitigate. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1:** Define EOT — equivalent SiO₂ thickness giving the same capacitance as the high-K stack. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Compute EOT of SiO₂ interface layer:
$$ EOT_{SiO_2} = \frac{3.9}{3.9}\cdot 0.6 = 0.6\,nm $$ **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3:** Compute EOT of HfO₂:
$$ EOT_{HfO_2} = \frac{3.9}{22}\cdot 2.5 = 0.443\,nm $$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Total EOT (series):
$$ EOT_{total} = 0.6 + 0.443 = \boxed{1.043\,nm} $$ **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 5:** C_ox:
$$ C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}\varepsilon_0}{EOT_{total}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot 8.854\times 10^{-12}}{1.043\times 10^{-9}} = 33.1\,mF/m^2 = \boxed{3.31\,\mu F/cm^2} $$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6:** Compare to pure 1.043 nm SiO₂: physical thickness in our stack = 0.6 + 2.5 = **3.1 nm** vs. 1.043 nm SiO₂. Using λ_T ≈ 0.6 nm and J_gate ∝ exp(−t_phys/λ_T):
$$ \frac{J_{SiO_2}}{J_{HK}} \approx \exp\left(\frac{3.1 - 1.043}{0.6}\right) = e^{3.43} \approx \boxed{30.9\times \text{ leakage reduction}} $$ **(2.5 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes — Avoid These:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1 + λV_DS) factor** — when asked for saturation I_D, students often give only the ideal square-law formula and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up λ and V_A** — remember: V_A = 1/λ, NOT V_A = λ. Examiners explicitly look for correct relationship.
> 3. **Using wrong κ in EOT** — EOT uses κ_SiO₂ = 3.9 in the numerator; the denominator is κ_high-K. Students sometimes invert this ratio.
> 4. **Forgetting series addition** — when stack has multiple layers, EOT_total = Σ EOT_i. Marks awarded for showing this additive property.
> 5. **Unit confusion in C_ox** — remember C_ox is capacitance *per unit area* (F/m²), not absolute capacitance. Examiners deduct if units are wrong.
> 6. **Skipping the saturation check (V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_th)** — always verify the region before applying the saturation formula.
> 7. **Confusing EOT with physical thickness** — EOT is *electrical*; physical thickness is real. Always state both when relevant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Channel Length Modulation — Key Takeaways
- ⭐ **Physical Origin:** Lateral expansion of drain-body depletion region into the channel for V_DS > V_DS,sat.
- ⭐ **Modified I_D Equation:** $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2(1+\lambda V_{DS})$
- ⭐ **λ ≈ (1/L)·√(2ε_si·ε_0/qN_A)** — inversely proportional to L; stronger CLM at shorter channels.
- ⭐ **Output Resistance:** $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$
- ⭐ **Early Voltage:** $V_A = 1/\lambda$ (intercept on negative V_DS axis when I_D–V_DS saturation line is extrapolated).
- ⭐ **Effect on Analog Gain:** Degrades intrinsic gain g_m·r_o; mitigated by cascode topology.
- ⭐ **Output Conductance:** $g_{ds} = \lambda I_D$
- ⭐ **I_D–V_DS curve in saturation:** Sloped line, slope = λ·I_D.
- ⭐ **Effect on Drain current:** For V_DS > V_DS,sat, I_D increases linearly with V_DS.

### High-K Gate Dielectrics & EOT — Key Takeaways
- ⭐ **EOT Definition:** Equivalent SiO₂ thickness giving the same C_ox as the high-K layer.
- ⭐ **EOT Formula (single layer):** $EOT = \dfrac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high-K}} \cdot t_{phys} = \dfrac{3.9}{\kappa_{high-K}} \cdot t_{phys}$
- ⭐ **EOT Formula (multi-layer stack):** $EOT_{total} = \sum EOT_i$ (series-capacitance additivity).
- ⭐ **C_ox using EOT:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\kappa_{SiO_2}\varepsilon_0}{EOT}$
- ⭐ **Common High-K Materials:** HfO₂ (κ ≈ 22), ZrO₂ (κ ≈ 25), La₂O₃ (κ ≈ 27), HfSiON (κ ≈ 15).
- ⭐ **Industry First:** Intel 45 nm node (2007) — first high-volume integration of Hf-based high-K + metal gate.
- ⭐ **Scaling Crisis:** SiO₂ below ~1.5 nm causes unacceptable direct tunneling leakage (>1 A/cm²).
- ⭐ **Modern Stack:** Metal Gate / High-K / Thin SiO₂ IL / Si Substrate.
- ⭐ **Physical thickness 5–10 nm of HfO₂** gives EOT < 1 nm with leakage reduction ~100×–1000×.
- ⭐ **Threshold voltage with EOT:** $V_{th} = \Phi_{MS} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}\varepsilon_0 q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$; smaller EOT → larger C_ox → smaller V_th variation (better short-channel control).
- ⭐ **Quantum Mechanical Tunneling:** $J_{gate} \propto \exp(-t_{phys}/\lambda_T)$ — thicker physical layer exponentially reduces leakage.

### Cross-Concept Synergy
- 🔗 Both CLM and EOT reflect the **inherent tension between scaling and device performance**: as devices shrink, second-order effects (CLM) and leakage (high-K) demand sophisticated engineering solutions.
- 🔗 Both are critical **SPICE model parameters** in modern compact models (BSIM-CMG, PSP, HiSIM).
- 🔗 Both are **examiner favorites** for 7-mark and 14-mark derivations in KTU ESE Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of High-K Gate Dielectrics on MOSFET Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT634 - NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effects of High-K Gate Dielectrics on MOSFET Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **High-K (High Dielectric Constant) Gate Dielectric** is an insulating material with a relative permittivity (dielectric constant, $\kappa$ or $k$) significantly greater than that of conventional silicon dioxide ($\kappa_{SiO_2} = 3.9$), deliberately integrated between the gate electrode and the silicon channel of a MOSFET to maintain or enhance gate capacitance while suppressing gate leakage current arising from quantum-mechanical direct tunneling.

The primary figure-of-merit used to compare high-K layers with SiO₂ is the **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)**, which represents the thickness of SiO₂ that would yield the same gate capacitance per unit area as the high-K dielectric being used.

$$EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{high\text{-}K}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Leaky Water-Pipe Analogy**

Imagine a municipal water pipe carrying pressurized water. If the pipe wall becomes paper-thin, water sprays out through microscopic cracks (leakage). You cannot easily increase pressure without strengthening the wall. The engineer’s smart solution is **not** to find a "stronger paper" but to **switch to a thicker but equally effective material** — say, reinforced rubber of the same hydraulic performance but greater mechanical robustness.

In the MOSFET world:
- **"Water pressure"** = the electric field driving current through the gate dielectric.
- **"Pipe wall"** = the gate oxide layer.
- **"Spraying water"** = **direct electron tunneling** (gate leakage).
- **"Reinforced rubber"** = **high-K material** (e.g., HfO₂) which is physically thicker but electrically equivalent to a much thinner SiO₂.

By replacing a thin SiO₂ layer (~1.2 nm) with a physically thicker HfO₂ layer (~5–7 nm) of the same EOT, tunneling leakage drops by **orders of magnitude (10³–10⁵×)** while the gate’s ability to control the channel remains intact.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Dielectric constant of SiO₂:** $k_{SiO_2} = 3.9$
- **Dielectric constant of Si₃N₄:** $k_{Si_3N_4} \approx 7.5$
- **Dielectric constant of HfO₂:** $k_{HfO_2} \approx 22\text{–}25$
- **Dielectric constant of ZrO₂:** $k_{ZrO_2} \approx 22\text{–}25$
- **Dielectric constant of Al₂O₃:** $k_{Al_2O_3} \approx 9$
- **Dielectric constant of La₂O₃:** $k_{La_2O_3} \approx 30$
- **Silicon bandgap:** $E_g^{Si} = 1.12$ eV
- **SiO₂ bandgap:** $E_g^{SiO_2} = 9$ eV
- **HfO₂ bandgap:** $E_g^{HfO_2} \approx 5.7$ eV

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 45 nm CMOS technology node (Intel, 2007) was the first commercial process to introduce Hf-based high-K dielectrics (HfSiON) with **EOT ≈ 1.0 nm**, replacing SiO₂. This is the canonical industry case study in KTU module evaluations.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Conventional SiO₂ Reaches Its Scaling Limit

The driving force for MOSFET miniaturization is the classical **Dennard scaling law**, which demands that oxide thickness ($t_{ox}$) shrink in proportion to channel length to preserve the **long-channel threshold-voltage condition**:

$$V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where

$$C_{ox} = \frac{k_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$$

For 90 nm and below nodes, $t_{ox}$ was pushed below **1.5 nm**, causing:

- **Exponential rise in direct tunneling current** ($J_{tunnel} \propto \exp(-\alpha \cdot t_{ox})$).
- **Quasi-ballistic gate leakage** through trapezoidal/triangular barriers.
- **Static power dissipation** becoming comparable to dynamic power — the famous **power wall** problem.
- **Boron penetration** from p⁺ poly-Si gates into the channel through ultra-thin SiO₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1.0 nm Tunneling Wall:** Below ~1.0 nm SiO₂ thickness, the gate leakage current exceeds the **maximum tolerable standby-power budget of ~1 A/cm²** for mobile/handheld CMOS. This is the hard physical limit beyond which SiO₂ alone cannot be scaled.

### 2.2 The High-K Solution — Physical Thickness vs. EOT

The fundamental insight is to **decouple electrical thickness (EOT) from physical thickness ($t_{phys}$)**.

$$t_{phys}^{high\text{-}K} = EOT \cdot \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}}{k_{SiO_2}}$$

Since $k_{high\text{-}K} \gg k_{SiO_2}$, we get $t_{phys} \gg EOT$. A thicker physical layer exponentially suppresses tunneling while preserving gate capacitance.

### 2.3 Positive Effects of High-K Dielectrics on MOSFET Performance

1. **Drastic reduction in gate leakage current (10³–10⁶× lower than SiO₂).**
2. **Maintained or improved drive current** through preserved $C_{ox}$.
3. **Better short-channel control** via equivalent thin EOT.
4. **Reduced static power consumption.**
5. **Compatibility with metal gate electrodes** (eliminating poly-Si depletion).
6. **Improved reliability under NBTI/PBTI stress** (when properly engineered).
7. **Higher transistor density** without thermal-runaway from leakage.

### 2.4 Adverse / Degrading Effects of High-K Dielectrics

| Adverse Effect | Physical Origin | Impact on MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility degradation** | Remote phonon scattering, Coulomb scattering from fixed/interface charges | Reduced $\mu_{eff}$, lower drive current $I_{on}$ |
| **Threshold voltage shift ($V_T$ instability)** | Fermi-level pinning at poly-Si/high-K interface; oxygen vacancies | Unwanted $V_T$ roll-off, asymmetric NMOS/PMOS |
| **Fixed charge and trapped charge** | Oxygen vacancies, bulk defects in HfO₂ | $V_T$ instability, hysteresis in $C\text{-}V$ |
| **Reduced bandgap / lower band offsets** | $E_g^{HfO_2} \approx 5.7$ eV (vs. 9 eV for SiO₂) | Lower barrier for electron/hole injection |
| **Poor thermal stability** | Crystallization of HfO₂ during high-T anneal | Grain boundaries → leakage paths |
| **Boron penetration** | B from p⁺ poly-Si diffuses into high-K | $V_T$ shift in PMOS |
| **Interfacial layer formation** | SiO₂-like IL grows between high-K and Si | EOT penalty (increases EOT) |
| **PBTI (Positive Bias Temp. Instability)** | Electron trapping in high-K bulk | Long-term reliability concern |

> [!WARNING]
> A thicker physical high-K layer **does not** automatically guarantee lower leakage if the **interfacial SiO₂-like layer** (IL) is too thin or if the high-K material crystallizes. The IL must typically remain **0.5–1.0 nm** for low interface trap density.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{k_{ox}\,\varepsilon_0\,A}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area when $A = 1$ |
| 2 | $EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \dfrac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{high\text{-}K}}$ | Equivalent Oxide Thickness definition |
| 3 | $t_{high\text{-}K} = EOT \cdot \dfrac{k_{high\text{-}K}}{k_{SiO_2}}$ | Required physical thickness of high-K |
| 4 | $J_{tunnel} \propto E_{ox}^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{4\sqrt{2m^*\,\phi_B}}{\hbar}\,t_{ox}\right)$ | Fowler-Nordheim / direct tunneling gate leakage |
| 5 | $V_T = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{Si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$ | Long-channel threshold voltage |
| 6 | $\mu_{eff} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta_1 E_{eff} + \theta_2 E_{eff}^2}$ | Effective channel mobility vs. effective field |
| 7 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox} W}{2L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | Saturation drain current (long-channel) |
| 8 | $SS = \ln(10) \cdot \dfrac{kT}{q}\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | Subthreshold swing (lower bound $60$ mV/dec) |
| 9 | $\Phi_{B,n} = \chi_{Si} + E_g^{Si} - \chi_{ox}$ | Schottky-type electron barrier to oxide |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** EOT is the **single most cited metric** in process technology roadmaps (ITRS/IRDS). Every advanced node (45 nm, 32 nm, 22 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm) is benchmarked against EOT targets ranging from **1.0 nm down to ~0.7 nm**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — EOT ↔ Physical Thickness Relationship

We start with the gate capacitance per unit area for a planar dielectric:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{k\,\varepsilon_0}{t}$$

For SiO₂ of thickness $t_{SiO_2}$:

$$C_{ox}^{SiO_2} = \frac{k_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{SiO_2}}$$

For a high-K dielectric of physical thickness $t_{high\text{-}K}$:

$$C_{ox}^{high\text{-}K} = \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

By **definition of EOT**, both produce the same capacitance:

$$C_{ox}^{SiO_2} = C_{ox}^{high\text{-}K}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{k_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

Cancel $\varepsilon_0$ and cross-multiply:

$$k_{SiO_2} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K} = k_{high\text{-}K} \cdot EOT$$

Solving for the physical thickness:

$$t_{high\text{-}K} = EOT \cdot \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}}{k_{SiO_2}}$$

Solving for EOT:

$$EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{high\text{-}K}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $k_{high\text{-}K} \gg k_{SiO_2}$, the **physical thickness $t_{high\text{-}K}$ is larger than the EOT**, which is the entire physical motivation behind replacing SiO₂ with high-K materials.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 45 nm node MOSFET requires an EOT of **1.0 nm**. Calculate the physical thickness of (a) SiO₂, (b) HfO₂ ($k = 25$), and (c) La₂O₃ ($k = 30$) needed to achieve this EOT.

#### (a) For SiO₂

$$t_{SiO_2} = EOT \cdot \frac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{SiO_2}} = 1.0 \cdot 1.0 = 1.0 \text{ nm}$$

This is below the tunneling wall — **not viable** in production.

#### (b) For HfO₂

$$t_{HfO_2} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{25}{3.9}$$

$$t_{HfO_2} = 6.41 \text{ nm}$$

#### (c) For La₂O₃

$$t_{La_2O_3} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{30}{3.9}$$

$$t_{La_2O_3} = 7.69 \text{ nm}$$

**Observation:** A 6–8 nm physical thickness layer of high-K material is **~6–8× thicker** than the equivalent SiO₂, drastically suppressing direct tunneling.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Leakage Current Reduction Estimate

For SiO₂ at $t_{ox} = 1.0$ nm under $V_{GS} = 1$ V, experimental data gives $J_{leak} \approx 100$ A/cm². For HfO₂ at the same EOT but $t_{phys} \approx 6.4$ nm, the leakage follows an exponential dependence on barrier width.

Using a simplified tunneling model (WKB approximation):

$$J_{tunnel} \propto \exp(-\kappa_B \cdot t_{phys})$$

where $\kappa_B = \dfrac{4\pi\sqrt{2m^*\phi_B}}{h}$ is the decay constant inside the barrier.

For SiO₂ at $t_{phys} = 1.0$ nm, $J_{leak} \approx 10^2$ A/cm².
For HfO₂ at $t_{phys} = 6.4$ nm, the leakage falls to $J_{leak} \approx 10^{-2}$ A/cm².

$$\text{Reduction factor} = \frac{10^2}{10^{-2}} = 10^{4}$$

This **four orders-of-magnitude reduction** is the headline advantage of high-K.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-offs

| Parameter | SiO₂ (1.0 nm) | HfO₂ (6.4 nm) | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical thickness | 1.0 nm | 6.4 nm | ↑ Tunneling suppression |
| Gate leakage | ~100 A/cm² | ~$10^{-2}$ A/cm² | ↓↓ by 10⁴× |
| $C_{ox}$ per unit area | High | Same EOT → same $C_{ox}$ | Neutral |
| Drive current $I_{on}$ | High | High (or slightly lower due to mobility) | Neutral / slight ↓ |
| Effective mobility $\mu_{eff}$ | High | Reduced (~30–50% drop) | ↓ Performance |
| Threshold voltage stability | Good | Poor (Fermi pinning) | ↓ Needs metal gate |
| Bandgap | 9 eV | 5.7 eV | ↓ Lower barrier |
| Thermal stability | Excellent | Poor (crystallizes ~600 °C) | ↓ Needs capping layer |
| NBTI reliability | Good | Moderate | Neutral |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**

**Define Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT). Why is it a more useful parameter than physical oxide thickness in modern MOSFET design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EOT is defined as the thickness of a SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the high-K dielectric being considered:

$$EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot \frac{k_{SiO_2}}{k_{high\text{-}K}}$$

EOT is more useful than physical thickness because it directly represents the **electrical effect** of the gate dielectric on the MOSFET — namely, the gate capacitance, threshold voltage, and short-channel control. Two different dielectrics with the same EOT will produce the same electrical behavior regardless of their physical thickness. This allows engineers to benchmark process technologies uniformly across the semiconductor industry (ITRS/IRDS roadmap uses EOT as the standard metric).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]**

**Why cannot SiO₂ be scaled below ~1.0 nm in advanced CMOS technology?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Below ~1.0 nm, SiO₂ behaves as a near-atomically-thin insulating film. Quantum-mechanical **direct tunneling** becomes dominant: electrons from the gate (or channel) tunnel directly through the SiO₂ trapezoidal barrier without overcoming it. The tunneling current density obeys an exponential dependence on oxide thickness and reaches values of **~10–100 A/cm²** at 1 V gate bias. This leakage:

1. Causes unacceptable **static power dissipation** (especially in mobile/IoT).
2. Causes **threshold voltage instability** due to charging of the thin oxide.
3. Leads to **Boron penetration** from p⁺ poly-Si gate into the channel.
4. Violates the **maximum standby-power budget** (~1 A/cm²) prescribed by ITRS.

Hence, high-K dielectrics (HfO₂, HfSiON) with thicker physical layers are mandatory for sub-45 nm nodes.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) Explain the limitations of SiO₂ as a gate dielectric in advanced MOSFETs. Discuss how high-K dielectrics overcome these limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The conventional gate dielectric for MOSFETs has been thermally grown SiO₂ for decades, owing to its excellent interface quality with silicon and its thermal stability. However, as CMOS technology progressed beyond the 90 nm node, several fundamental limitations emerged:

**1. Quantum-Mechanical Tunneling Leakage:**
When $t_{SiO_2}$ falls below ~1.5 nm, the gate insulator becomes so thin that carriers (electrons/holes) from the silicon channel can quantum-mechanically tunnel through the forbidden energy gap of SiO₂. This **direct tunneling** current rises exponentially with decreasing thickness:

$$J_{tunnel} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\pi\sqrt{2m^*\phi_B}}{h}\,t_{ox}\right)$$

For $t_{ox} = 1.0$ nm, $J_{leak} \approx 100$ A/cm², exceeding the acceptable standby-power budget.

**2. Boron Penetration:**
In pMOSFETs with p⁺ poly-Si gates, **boron dopants diffuse through ultra-thin SiO₂** into the channel during high-temperature anneals, causing severe $V_T$ shifts and degrading device matching.

**3. Poly-Si Gate Depletion:**
The heavily-doped poly-Si gate experiences carrier depletion at the gate-oxide interface under inversion, effectively increasing the EOT by ~0.3–0.5 nm and reducing drive current.

**4. Static Power Crisis:**
Tunneling leakage at sub-1.5 nm SiO₂ causes standby power to exceed dynamic switching power, violating the Dennard scaling assumption.

**How High-K Overcomes These Limitations:**

- **Decoupling of EOT from physical thickness:** A high-K material (e.g., HfO₂ with $k \approx 25$) achieves the same capacitance with ~6× the physical thickness, exponentially suppressing tunneling.
- **Compatibility with metal gates:** High-K + metal gate stacks eliminate poly-Si depletion.
- **Reduced boron penetration** due to denser atomic structure and shorter anneal budgets.
- **Orders-of-magnitude leakage reduction (10³–10⁶×)** while preserving short-channel control.

---

**(b) With a suitable diagram, explain the structure of a modern MOSFET gate stack employing a high-K dielectric. List any four high-K materials commonly used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure:** A modern MOSFET gate stack consists of:

1. **Metal gate electrode** (e.g., TiN, TaN, W) — replaces poly-Si to eliminate depletion.
2. **High-K dielectric layer** (e.g., HfO₂, ~2–4 nm).
3. **Interfacial layer (IL)** — a thin (~0.5–1.0 nm) SiO₂-like layer between high-K and Si channel for low interface trap density.
4. **Silicon channel** (the inversion layer forms here under gate bias).
5. **Source and drain** regions (raised or conventional).

**Typical Stack (top to bottom):**
Metal Gate → High-K Dielectric (HfO₂) → Interfacial Layer (SiO₂-like) → Silicon Channel

**Four Common High-K Materials:**

| Material | $k$ value | Bandgap (eV) | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HfO₂** | 22–25 | 5.7 | Industry-standard for 45 nm and beyond |
| **ZrO₂** | 22–25 | 5.8 | Similar to HfO₂; less mature |
| **Al₂O₃** | 9 | 8.7 | Larger bandgap, good barrier |
| **HfSiO(N)** | 10–15 | 6–7 | Silicon-doped HfO₂; resists crystallization |
| **La₂O₃** | 27–30 | 5.5 | Highest $k$; poor stability |

**Process Flow Note:** The high-K layer is typically deposited by **Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD)** for sub-nm thickness control.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a) Derive the relationship between the physical thickness of a high-K dielectric and its Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT). A 32 nm MOSFET requires an EOT of 0.8 nm. Calculate the physical thickness of (i) HfO₂ ($k = 25$) and (ii) Al₂O₃ ($k = 9$) needed to achieve this EOT. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The gate capacitance per unit area for any dielectric of thickness $t$ and dielectric constant $k$ is:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{k\,\varepsilon_0}{t}$$

For a SiO₂ layer of thickness $EOT$:

$$C_{ox}^{SiO_2} = \frac{k_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT}$$

For a high-K layer of physical thickness $t_{high\text{-}K}$:

$$C_{ox}^{high\text{-}K} = \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

By definition of EOT, both produce the **same capacitance**:

$$\frac{k_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}\,\varepsilon_0}{t_{high\text{-}K}}$$

Canceling $\varepsilon_0$:

$$k_{SiO_2} \cdot t_{high\text{-}K} = k_{high\text{-}K} \cdot EOT$$

Solving for the physical thickness:

$$\boxed{t_{high\text{-}K} = EOT \cdot \frac{k_{high\text{-}K}}{k_{SiO_2}}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $EOT = 0.8$ nm, $k_{SiO_2} = 3.9$.

**(i) For HfO₂ ($k = 25$):**

$$t_{HfO_2} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{25}{3.9}$$

$$t_{HfO_2} = 0.8 \cdot 6.41 = 5.13 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) For Al₂O₃ ($k = 9$):**

$$t_{Al_2O_3} = 0.8 \cdot \frac{9}{3.9}$$

$$t_{Al_2O_3} = 0.8 \cdot 2.31 = 1.85 \text{ nm}$$

**Result:** HfO₂ provides a much thicker physical barrier (5.13 nm vs. 1.85 nm) at the same EOT, hence superior tunneling suppression.

---

**(b) Explain the major challenges associated with integrating high-K dielectrics in MOSFETs. Discuss how the industry has addressed each of these issues. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

While high-K dielectrics solve the leakage crisis, they introduce **significant integration challenges**:

**1. Reduced Channel Mobility:**
The high-K material is physically separated from the channel by only a thin interfacial layer. Charge carriers in the channel experience additional **remote phonon scattering** (dipoles in the high-K vibrate) and **Coulomb scattering** (from fixed charges in the high-K). This reduces effective mobility $\mu_{eff}$ by 30–50%.
**Industry Solution:** Si-doping of HfO₂ (forming HfSiON) partially passivates dipoles and reduces scattering. Also, careful IL engineering preserves mobility.

**2. Threshold Voltage Instability / Fermi-Level Pinning:**
At the interface between poly-Si and high-K, **Fermi-level pinning** causes $V_T$ to shift unpredictably, with NMOS and PMOS requiring very different gate work-functions.
**Industry Solution:** Complete replacement of poly-Si with **metal gates** (TiN, TaN, W) whose work-functions can be tuned via doping (e.g., TiAlC for NMOS, TiN for PMOS).

**3. Thermal Instability and Crystallization:**
HfO₂ crystallizes at ~600 °C, creating grain boundaries that serve as leakage paths.
**Industry Solution:** Add **HfSiO** or **HfSiON** (Si or N doping) to raise crystallization temperature above 1000 °C. Use **amorphous capping layers**.

**4. Reduced Bandgap and Band Offsets:**
HfO₂'s bandgap (~5.7 eV) and conduction/valence band offsets to Si are smaller than SiO₂, increasing thermionic emission over the barrier.
**Industry Solution:** Choose nitrided high-K (HfSiON) which slightly widens the bandgap. Combine with metal gate to lower tunneling probability.

**5. Interfacial Layer Regrowth:**
After high-K deposition, oxygen can diffuse through the high-K during anneals and regrow a thick SiO₂-like IL at the Si interface, **inflating the EOT** and defeating the purpose.
**Industry Solution:** Use **nitrogen-rich ILs** or process high-K first then surface-treat to suppress regrowth.

**6. Reliability Issues (PBTI, NBTI, TDDB):**
Electron trapping in bulk high-K defects and stress-induced leakage current limit device lifetime.
**Industry Solution:** Process optimization, defect passivation via Si-doping, and adoption of stress-relaxed geometries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> - **Do NOT confuse EOT with physical thickness** — they are only equal when $k = 3.9$ (i.e., for SiO₂).
> - **Do NOT state that high-K materials have higher mobility** — they REDUCE mobility due to remote phonon scattering. This is a frequent trap.
> - **Always mention the interfacial layer (IL)** — examiners explicitly look for this in 7-mark answers.
> - **Mention Fermi-level pinning** when discussing $V_T$ instability; do not attribute it solely to fixed charges.
> - **Numerical questions:** show the EOT formula derivation first, then substitute; do not skip cancellation steps.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

- **EOT definition:** $EOT = t_{high\text{-}K} \cdot (k_{SiO_2}/k_{high\text{-}K})$ — fundamental identity.
- **Physical thickness formula:** $t_{high\text{-}K} = EOT \cdot (k_{high\text{-}K}/k_{SiO_2})$.
- **SiO₂ tunneling limit:** $t_{ox} \approx 1.0$ nm causes >10 A/cm² leakage — the **1 nm wall**.
- **High-K advantage:** Same $C_{ox}$ at ~5–8× thicker physical layer → **10³–10⁶× lower leakage**.
- **Standard high-K materials:** HfO₂ (k≈25), HfSiON (k≈12–15), ZrO₂ (k≈25), Al₂O₃ (k≈9), La₂O₃ (k≈30).
- **Industry milestone:** Intel 45 nm node (2007) — first high-K + metal gate (HfSiON + TiN).
- **Major demerits:** mobility degradation (30–50%), $V_T$ instability, Fermi-level pinning, thermal instability, smaller bandgap.
- **Key ratios to memorize:** $k_{SiO_2} = 3.9$, $k_{HfO_2} \approx 25$ → ratio ≈ **6.41** (used in every numerical problem).
- **Bandgap values:** $E_g^{SiO_2} = 9$ eV vs. $E_g^{HfO_2} = 5.7$ eV.
- **Interfacial layer (IL):** always ~0.5–1 nm SiO₂-like; non-negotiable for low $D_{it}$.
- **Process of choice for high-K deposition:** Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) — sub-monolayer control.
- **Dependence:** $C_{ox} \propto 1/t_{ox}$; reducing EOT increases $C_{ox}$ and drive current.
- **Poly-Si depletion** is fixed only by switching to **metal gates** — a co-evolution with high-K.
- **Trade-off mantra:** *Higher k → better EOT scaling → more phonon scattering → lower mobility.* This is the eternal engineering trade-off.
